addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)
- contribute:deep-reviewer: Agent from contribute plugin (Tools: All tools)
- contribute:issue-scout: Agent from contribute plugin (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- artifact-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use when building web components, pages, or applications. Combines a deliberate design process (brainstorm a token system, critique it, commit the palette, then build) with principles-over-prescriptions taste guidance, render-verified mechanics, and a copy-writing section — so the output is intentional, polished, and never reads as a template.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Let me start by scaffolding the directory and checking the toolchain.

content.text: Python 3.11.9 is available and the skeleton exists. Now I'll launch a large parallel workflow to author every concept file (with diagrams, Mermaid flowcharts, FENs, exercises), with an adversarial chess-accuracy verification pass over the concrete tactics and endgame files. It runs in the background while I build the top-level index, the SVG board generator, and showcase images.

input.script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'chess-grandmaster-curriculum',
  description: 'Author a complete 1500 to 2800 FIDE chess curriculum: tactics, strategy, endgames, openings, plans, with diagrams, Mermaid flowcharts and exercises; verify chess accuracy of concrete files.',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Tactics' },
    { title: 'Verify Tactics' },
    { title: 'Endgames' },
    { title: 'Verify Endgames' },
    { title: 'Strategy' },
    { title: 'Openings' },
    { title: 'Calculation' },
    { title: 'Mindset' },
    { title: 'Attack and Defense' },
    { title: 'Training Plan' },
    { title: 'Psychology and Start' },
    { title: 'Flowcharts' },
    { title: 'Folder Indexes' },
  ],
}

const ROOT = 'C:/GrandMaster/'
const name = p => p.split('/').pop()

const SG = `You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.`

const VERIFY = `You are a meticulous chess fact-checker (FIDE 2400+). Read the markdown file at the given absolute path, then verify EVERY ASCII board diagram, FEN and analysis line:
- Each ASCII board must EXACTLY match its FEN (piece types and exact files/ranks; UPPERCASE=White, lowercase=black, '.'=empty; rank 8 on top, rank 1 at bottom; 8 cells per row).
- Each FEN must be legal: exactly one king per side, no pawns on the 1st or 8th rank, a plausible legal position.
- Each stated tactic/mate/winning line must be SOUND. If a line is unsound or a position is illegal, FIX it: replace with a correct minimal position illustrating the SAME theme and update the ASCII board, FEN, Lichess link and analysis to match.
- Mermaid blocks must parse: first content line "flowchart TD", arrows -->, no parentheses/quotes/colons inside node labels.
- Notation must be correct algebraic.
Make fixes IN PLACE with the Edit tool. Do not rewrite good content or change structure. Reply with a short bullet list of fixes made, or "No changes needed".`

const makePrompt = s => `${SG}

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: ${s.topic}
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: ${s.hints}
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): ${ROOT}${s.path}`

const W = (path, topic, hints) => ({ path, topic, hints })

// ---------------- TACTICS ----------------
const TACTICS = [
  W('03-Tactics/fork.md', 'The Fork (one piece attacking two or more enemy targets at once)', 'Cover knight forks including the royal fork on king+queen, the family fork (K+Q+R), pawn forks, bishop/rook/queen forks. Use a minimal position of a white knight forking the black king and rook, and a pawn forking two pieces.'),
  W('03-Tactics/pin.md', 'The Pin (a piece cannot or should not move because a more valuable piece sits behind it)', 'Distinguish ABSOLUTE pin (against the king, illegal to move) vs RELATIVE pin. Show a bishop pinning a knight to the king (e.g. Bg5 vs Nf6 with Ke8 style), and "piling up on the pinned piece" / pin-to-win. Mention not to leave pieces pinned.'),
  W('03-Tactics/skewer.md', 'The Skewer (the reverse pin: a valuable piece is attacked and forced to move, exposing a lesser piece behind it)', 'Use a rook or bishop skewering the black king and queen on a line, winning the queen. Show a minimal R+K vs K+Q skewer and a bishop skewer on a diagonal.'),
  W('03-Tactics/discovered-attack.md', 'The Discovered Attack (moving one piece unveils an attack from a piece behind it)', 'Show a discovered attack where moving a knight/bishop unveils a rook or queen attack, often with the moving piece ALSO making a threat (double threat). Keep minimal.'),
  W('03-Tactics/discovered-check.md', 'Discovered Check and Double Check', 'Explain discovered check (the unveiled piece gives check while the moving piece grabs material or makes a threat) and DOUBLE CHECK (both pieces give check, king MUST move). Note double check is the most forcing move. Link to windmill and smothered mate.'),
  W('03-Tactics/double-attack.md', 'The Double Attack (creating two simultaneous threats so the opponent cannot meet both)', 'Broader than the fork: includes a queen attacking two loose pieces, a check that also attacks a piece, and threats of mate + material. Relate to the fork (fork = double attack by one piece). Geometry of loose pieces ("LPDO - loose pieces drop off").'),
  W('03-Tactics/decoy.md', 'The Decoy (attraction: luring an enemy piece, often the king, to a bad square via a sacrifice)', 'Classic: sacrifice to drag the king onto a square where a knight fork or check wins the queen. Show a queen or rook sacrifice that decoys the king, then a knight fork. Distinguish from deflection (decoy lures TO a square; deflection lures AWAY).'),
  W('03-Tactics/deflection.md', 'Deflection (luring or forcing a defender away from a key duty)', 'Show a back-rank deflection: deflect the rook/queen that guards the back rank, then mate. Also a deflection that removes the guard of a key square or piece. Contrast with decoy and removing-the-defender.'),
  W('03-Tactics/removing-the-defender.md', 'Removing the Defender (eliminating the piece that guards a key square, piece or mating square)', 'Capture or chase the defender, then execute the threat (mate or win material). Show capturing a knight that defends a back-rank mating square.'),
  W('03-Tactics/overloading.md', 'The Overloaded Piece (a defender tasked with too many duties)', 'A piece guarding two things at once; attack one duty and the other collapses. Minimal example with one rook defending both a back-rank square and a hanging piece.'),
  W('03-Tactics/interference.md', 'Interference and Obstruction (interposing a piece to cut the line between a defender and what it defends)', 'Block the defensive line, often with a sacrifice, so a piece is no longer guarded. Classic interference motif.'),
  W('03-Tactics/clearance-sacrifice.md', 'Clearance and Line-Opening Sacrifices (vacating a square, file, rank or diagonal for another piece)', 'Sacrifice or move a piece to clear a key square or open a line so another piece delivers the blow. Include square-clearance and line-clearance.'),
  W('03-Tactics/zwischenzug.md', 'Zwischenzug / Intermezzo (an in-between move inserted before the expected recapture)', 'Insert a more forcing move (a check or bigger threat) before recapturing, changing the outcome. Minimal example where a check wins a tempo/piece before recapture.'),
  W('03-Tactics/x-ray.md', 'The X-ray (a long-range piece acts through an enemy or friendly piece on a line)', 'X-ray attack and X-ray defense: a rook/queen/bishop defends or attacks a square THROUGH an intervening piece. Distinguish from skewer.'),
  W('03-Tactics/windmill.md', 'The Windmill / See-saw (a repeating discovered-check mechanism that harvests material)', 'Explain with the famous Carlos Torre vs Emanuel Lasker, Moscow 1925 windmill (rook + bishop see-saw on the 7th rank). Show the mechanism with a minimal diagram and the move sequence Bf6 then Rxg7+ Kh8 Rxf7+ etc.'),
  W('03-Tactics/trapped-piece.md', 'Trapping a Piece (restricting an enemy piece until it can be captured)', 'Trapped bishop (e.g. greedy bishop captures a pawn and gets trapped), trapped rook, trapped queen. Show a knight or pawns trapping a bishop on the rim.'),
  W('03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md', 'The Back-Rank Mate and Back-Rank Weakness', 'Mate on the 8th/1st rank when the king is boxed in by its own pawns. Show R/Q delivering Re8# behind a wall of pawns; explain "luft" (making an escape square h3/h6). Include a deflection-to-back-rank combo.'),
  W('03-Tactics/smothered-mate.md', 'Smothered Mate (the king is mated by a knight while hemmed in by its own pieces)', 'Show Philidor legacy pattern: Qg8+ (or Qd8 style) Rxg8, Nf7# with the king on h8 surrounded by g7/h7 pawns and the rook on g8/f8. Provide the exact minimal position and the forcing sequence.'),
  W('03-Tactics/desperado.md', 'The Desperado (a doomed piece sells itself as dearly as possible)', 'A piece that is going to be lost grabs material or creates a threat first; also mutual-capture sequences where you grab an extra pawn with a piece that is lost anyway. Minimal example.'),
  W('03-Tactics/underpromotion.md', 'Underpromotion Tactics (promoting to knight, rook or bishop instead of a queen)', 'Knight underpromotion with check/fork, rook underpromotion to avoid stalemate, bishop underpromotion. Show the classic knight-promotion-with-fork and a rook-to-avoid-stalemate example.'),
  W('03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md', 'Combinations and Sacrifices (chaining motifs into a forcing sequence)', 'How combinations are built from forcing moves (checks, captures, threats), real vs sham sacrifices, and calculating to a clear gain. Reference a short famous combination. Emphasize CCT - checks, captures, threats.'),
  W('03-Tactics/mating-patterns.md', 'Essential Mating Patterns and Mating Nets', 'Catalogue the key named mates with minimal diagrams: back rank, smothered, Anastasia, Arabian, Boden, Damiano, Greco, Legal, ladder/two-rook, Q+K basic mate. One tiny diagram + idea for each of several patterns.'),
]

// ---------------- ENDGAMES ----------------
const ENDGAMES = [
  W('05-Endgames/king-and-pawn-endgames.md', 'King and Pawn Endgames (the foundation of all endgame technique)', 'Cover the basic K+P vs K win/draw, the role of the king, when a pawn queens. Reference opposition, key squares, the rule of the square. Use the canonical white K+P vs black K positions.'),
  W('05-Endgames/the-opposition.md', 'The Opposition (kings facing off with one square between them; the side NOT to move often wins the key squares)', 'Direct, distant and diagonal opposition. Show the textbook K+P vs K position where taking the opposition wins or draws. Minimal kings-and-one-pawn diagrams.'),
  W('05-Endgames/triangulation.md', 'Triangulation (a king maneuver to lose a tempo and pass the move to the opponent, winning the opposition / a zugzwang)', 'THE classic triangulation: White king triangulates (e.g. Kd5-d4-e5 or e5-d5-e4) to reach the same position with Black to move, forcing zugzwang in a K+P endgame. Give the exact position (e.g. white Kc4 pawn e5? prefer a clean known triangulation position) with the move sequence and a clear before/after. Connect to opposition and zugzwang.'),
  W('05-Endgames/zugzwang.md', 'Zugzwang (any move worsens the position; the obligation to move is a liability)', 'Define zugzwang, mutual zugzwang (reciprocal), and how it powers king-and-pawn endgames and many wins. Show a clean zugzwang where whoever moves loses. Connect to triangulation and opposition.'),
  W('05-Endgames/key-squares-and-rule-of-the-square.md', 'Key Squares and the Rule of the Square', 'Key squares for K+P endings (the squares the king must reach to win), and the rule of the square for whether a lone king can catch a passed pawn. Show both with diagrams.'),
  W('05-Endgames/rook-endgames.md', 'Rook Endgames: Lucena, Philidor, the 7th Rank and Rook Activity', 'The two pillars: LUCENA position (building a bridge to promote with R+P vs R) and PHILIDOR position (passive defense on the 3rd rank then check from behind). Give the canonical positions and method for each. Add the principle "rooks belong behind passed pawns" and activity over material.'),
  W('05-Endgames/rook-vs-pawn.md', 'Rook vs Pawn(s) Endgames', 'When the rook stops the pawn and when the pawn (with king support) draws or wins; the "short side / long side" idea and cutting off the king. Minimal diagrams.'),
  W('05-Endgames/queen-vs-pawn.md', 'Queen vs Pawn on the 7th Rank', 'How the queen wins vs a rook/knight pawn but often only draws vs a bishop or rook pawn on the 7th (the stalemate defense). Show the zig-zag technique to bring the king up, and the drawing exceptions (a- and c-pawn / bishop and rook pawns).'),
  W('05-Endgames/minor-piece-endgames.md', 'Minor-Piece Endgames Overview (bishop vs knight, same-color and opposite)', 'When the bishop beats the knight (open positions, pawns on both wings) vs when the knight is better (closed, pawns on one side, fixed targets). Include the wrong-rook-pawn-and-bishop draw note.'),
  W('05-Endgames/opposite-colored-bishops.md', 'Opposite-Colored Bishop Endgames (famous for their drawish tendency, deadly in the middlegame)', 'Why two extra pawns can still be a draw with opposite bishops; the defender builds a blockade on its color; in the middlegame the attacker often wins because the defending bishop cannot cover the attacked color. Minimal diagrams.'),
  W('05-Endgames/the-fortress.md', 'The Fortress (a drawn setup the stronger side cannot break despite material deficit)', 'Define fortress, show one classic fortress (e.g. wrong bishop + rook pawn, or a R vs Q fortress idea). Keep the example clean and correct.'),
  W('05-Endgames/converting-advantages.md', 'Converting an Advantage (technique: trade pieces not pawns when ahead, the two-weaknesses principle, do not rush)', 'Practical conversion technique: simplify toward winning endgames, avoid counterplay, improve the worst piece, create a second weakness. Link to strategy two-weaknesses file.'),
  W('05-Endgames/pawn-breakthrough.md', 'The Pawn Breakthrough (sacrificing pawns to create an unstoppable passer)', 'The classic 3-vs-3 (or 3 connected passed pawns) breakthrough where a pawn sacrifice clears the way to promote. Show the textbook b/c/a-file breakthrough position and the sequence.'),
]

// ---------------- STRATEGY ----------------
const STRATEGY = [
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/pawn-structures.md', 'Pawn Structures (the skeleton that dictates the plans for both sides)', 'Overview of major structures: IQP, hanging pawns, Carlsbad, Maroczy bind, Stonewall, fixed chains (French/KID). For each, the typical plans. Diagrams of 2-3 structures.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/isolated-doubled-backward-pawns.md', 'Pawn Weaknesses: Isolated, Doubled and Backward Pawns', 'Define each weakness, why it is weak (the square in front, blockade), and when it is actually fine (dynamic IQP, doubled pawns controlling key squares, half-open file). Diagrams.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/passed-pawns.md', 'Passed Pawns (a pawn with no enemy pawns to stop it on its file or adjacent files)', 'Blockading, the "passed pawns must be pushed" idea, protected and connected passers, outside passed pawn as a decoy. Diagrams.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/weak-squares-and-outposts.md', 'Weak Squares and Outposts (a square that cannot be defended by a pawn, ideal for a knight)', 'Identifying holes, planting a knight on an outpost supported by a pawn, especially on the 5th/6th rank. Diagrams of a knight outpost.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/open-and-half-open-files.md', 'Open and Half-Open Files (the highways for rooks)', 'Seizing open files, doubling rooks, the relationship to the 7th rank, half-open files as attack lines. Diagrams.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/the-bishop-pair.md', 'The Bishop Pair (two bishops as a long-term advantage, especially in open positions)', 'Why two bishops are worth roughly half a pawn extra, how to open the position to exploit them, and how the defender neutralizes them. Diagram.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/good-and-bad-bishops.md', 'Good Bishop vs Bad Bishop (a bishop blocked by its own pawns)', 'Recognizing the bad bishop (pawns on its color), how to improve it (trade it, or reroute it outside the chain), and how to fix the opponent pawns to make their bishop bad. Diagrams.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/knight-vs-bishop.md', 'Knight vs Bishop (the eternal imbalance)', 'When the knight is better (closed centers, fixed pawns, strong outpost) vs the bishop (open, both wings, long diagonals). How to steer toward your favorable minor piece. Diagrams.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/the-center-and-space.md', 'The Center and Space Advantage', 'Types of centers (classical, open, closed, dynamic/mobile), the value of space, do not let space lull you into passivity, the defender should seek exchanges to relieve cramped positions. Diagrams.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/the-minority-attack.md', 'The Minority Attack (advancing the pawn minority to create a weakness in the enemy majority)', 'The classic Carlsbad/QGD minority attack: White plays b4-b5 to create a backward c6 pawn or a weak square. Show the structure and plan with a diagram.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/prophylaxis-and-restriction.md', 'Prophylaxis and Restriction (preventing the opponent plan before pursuing your own)', 'Nimzowitsch/Karpov style: ask "what does my opponent want?" and stop it; restrain enemy pieces and pawn breaks. Examples of prophylactic moves like h3, Kb1, a4.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/piece-activity-and-coordination.md', 'Piece Activity and Coordination (every piece on its best square, working together)', 'The principle of the worst-placed piece, harmony, centralization, rerouting maneuvers (e.g. Nf3-d2-c4 or Nb1-d2-f1-g3). Diagrams.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/king-safety-and-pawn-shields.md', 'King Safety and the Pawn Shield', 'Evaluating king safety, weaknesses created by pawn moves in front of the king (h3/g3 holes), opposite-side castling implications, when to keep the king in the center. Diagrams.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/the-two-weaknesses-principle.md', 'The Principle of Two Weaknesses', 'A single weakness is often defensible; create a SECOND front so the defense is overstretched. The classic conversion technique. Diagram and a model plan.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/exchanges-and-when-to-trade.md', 'Exchanges: When and What to Trade', 'Trade when ahead in material, trade the opponent good pieces, keep pieces when attacking or when you have more space; the difference between trading toward a won endgame vs toward a draw. Guidelines + diagram.'),
  W('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/maneuvering-and-improving-pieces.md', 'Maneuvering and Improving Your Position (playing without an immediate target)', 'How strong players improve slowly: regroup, provoke weaknesses, build up before breaking, the art of doing-nothing-useful-for-the-opponent. Example maneuvers and a diagram.'),
]

// ---------------- OPENINGS ----------------
const OPENINGS = [
  W('06-Openings/opening-principles.md', 'Opening Principles (develop, control the center, castle, connect rooks, do not move the same piece twice)', 'The timeless principles, common opening mistakes (early queen, too many pawn moves, neglecting development), and how principles translate to plans. One annotated short opening.'),
  W('06-Openings/building-a-repertoire.md', 'Building an Opening Repertoire (choosing systems that suit your style and grow with you)', 'How to pick White and Black repertoires, depth over breadth, learning ideas not just moves, using a database, and how repertoire needs change from 1500 to 2800.'),
  W('06-Openings/open-games-1e4-e5.md', 'Open Games after 1.e4 e5 (Italian, Ruy Lopez, Scotch, Petroff, and the gambits)', 'Key ideas of the Italian (slow buildup, d3/c3-d4), Ruy Lopez (pressure on e5, the closed center), Scotch, Petroff. Typical plans and one diagram of a main tabiya.'),
  W('06-Openings/the-sicilian-and-semi-open-games.md', 'The Sicilian and Other Semi-Open Defenses (Caro-Kann, French, Scandinavian, Pirc)', 'Why 1...c5 fights for the win, Open vs Closed Sicilian ideas, and the character of the French (good/bad bishop), Caro-Kann (solid), Scandinavian, Pirc. Diagrams of typical structures.'),
  W('06-Openings/queens-gambit-and-d4-structures.md', 'The Queens Gambit and 1.d4 Structures (QGD, QGA, Slav, Carlsbad)', 'Central control with d4/c4, the QGD/Slav/QGA character, the Carlsbad structure and minority attack, the role of the c8 bishop problem. Diagrams.'),
  W('06-Openings/indian-defenses-and-fianchetto.md', 'Indian Defenses and the Fianchetto (Nimzo, Queens Indian, Kings Indian, Gruenfeld, Catalan)', 'Hypermodern ideas: cede the center then strike it; Nimzo (doubling White pawns), KID (kingside pawn storm vs queenside play), Gruenfeld (pressure on the big center), the fianchetto bishop. Diagrams.'),
  W('06-Openings/gambits-traps-and-pitfalls.md', 'Gambits, Traps and Common Opening Pitfalls', 'Sound vs dubious gambits, famous traps (Legal mate, Fried Liver, Noahs Ark, the Lasker trap), and how not to fall for or over-rely on traps. Diagrams of 2 famous traps with correct lines.'),
]

// ---------------- CALCULATION / BOARD VISION ----------------
const CALC = [
  W('02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/visualization-training.md', 'Board Vision and Visualization Training (seeing the board and squares without moving pieces)', 'Knowing square colors, knight-distance, blindfold mini-drills, the "calculate without moving the pieces" rule, coordinate drills. Concrete daily exercises.'),
  W('02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/calculation-method.md', 'A Method for Calculation (forcing moves first, candidate moves, the tree, when to stop)', 'Kotov tree of analysis (with its criticisms), Aagaard candidate-move method, checks-captures-threats, calculating only forcing lines deeply. Worked calculation on a tactical position.'),
  W('02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/candidate-moves-and-the-tree.md', 'Candidate Moves and Managing the Analysis Tree', 'How to generate candidate moves, prune, avoid analysis tree chaos, the "look at ALL checks and captures" habit, comparison method. Diagram with several candidates.'),
  W('02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/evaluating-positions.md', 'Evaluating a Position (material, king safety, activity, pawn structure, initiative)', 'The evaluation checklist, static vs dynamic factors, when activity beats material, forming a plan from the evaluation. Worked evaluation of a diagram.'),
  W('02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/prophylactic-and-defensive-calculation.md', 'Defensive Calculation and Prophylaxis at the Board', 'Calculating the opponent threats, the "is it safe?" check before committing, finding the only-move defense, not hanging pieces in your own calculation. Examples.'),
]

// ---------------- MINDSET / STUDY ----------------
const MINDSET = [
  W('01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/deliberate-practice.md', 'Deliberate Practice for Chess (how skill is actually built)', 'Ericsson deliberate practice applied to chess: work at the edge of ability, immediate feedback, focused reps, why mindless blitz does not improve you. A concrete deliberate-practice loop.'),
  W('01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/building-a-study-routine.md', 'Building a Sustainable Study Routine', 'How to split time across tactics, endgames, openings, games and analysis at different levels; the 40/30/30 type guidance; consistency over cramming; sample weekly hours by rating.'),
  W('01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/analyzing-your-own-games.md', 'Analyzing Your Own Games (the single highest-value habit)', 'Annotate before the engine, find the critical moments, classify mistakes (tactical, strategic, time, psychological), keep an error log, only THEN check the engine. A step-by-step process.'),
  W('01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/using-engines-databases-and-tablebases.md', 'Using Engines, Databases and Tablebases Wisely', 'Engines for verification not for thinking, how to query a database for opening/middlegame plans, when tablebases settle an endgame, the trap of engine-dependence. Practical workflow.'),
  W('01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/learning-from-master-games.md', 'Learning from Master and Model Games', 'How to study annotated classics, guess-the-move training, choosing model games for your openings/structures, building pattern knowledge. A short study protocol and recommended collections.'),
]

// ---------------- ATTACK & DEFENSE ----------------
const ATKDEF = [
  W('07-Attacking-Play/attacking-the-castled-king.md', 'Attacking the Castled King (opening lines toward the king)', 'Principles: bring more attackers than defenders, open files/diagonals, the pawn storm, remove key defenders (the f6/Nf6 defender). Diagram of a typical kingside attack setup.'),
  W('07-Attacking-Play/the-greek-gift-bxh7.md', 'The Greek Gift Sacrifice (Bxh7+)', 'The classic bishop sac on h7 followed by Ng5+ and Qh5, when it works (pawn on e5, knight ready for g5, queen access to h-file, no defender of h7). Give the exact thematic position, the main lines after Kg8, Kg6 and Kh6, and when it fails.'),
  W('07-Attacking-Play/sacrifices-on-g7-h6-and-the-fianchetto.md', 'Sacrifices on g7, h6 and against the Fianchetto', 'Demolishing the fianchetto with Bxh6/Bxg7 and the exchange sac Rxc3, opening the king. When the dark squares collapse. Diagrams.'),
  W('07-Attacking-Play/opposite-side-castling-and-pawn-storms.md', 'Opposite-Side Castling and Pawn Storms (the race)', 'When kings castle on opposite wings, both sides storm pawns; principles of the race: do not move your own king pawns, open a file, speed and tempo matter most. Diagram.'),
  W('07-Attacking-Play/piece-sacrifices-for-initiative.md', 'Sacrificing for the Initiative and Compensation', 'Long-term (positional) sacrifices for development, the initiative, weak squares or a bind; how to judge compensation when there is no forced win. Examples.'),
  W('08-Defense-and-Counterattack/principles-of-defense.md', 'Principles of Defense (the hardest skill)', 'Trade attackers, find the only move, return material to break the attack, do not panic, prophylaxis, create a fortress; defend actively when possible. Examples.'),
  W('08-Defense-and-Counterattack/the-art-of-counterattack.md', 'The Art of the Counterattack (the best defense is often offense)', 'Meeting a flank attack with a central break, counterattacking the overextended attacker, tempo and the in-between counterthreat. Examples.'),
  W('08-Defense-and-Counterattack/defending-worse-positions-and-saving-draws.md', 'Defending Worse Positions and Saving Draws', 'Tenacity, the swindle, setting practical problems, stalemate and fortress resources, perpetual check; the psychology of defense. Examples including a perpetual check and a stalemate trick.'),
]

// ---------------- TRAINING PLAN (rating bands) ----------------
const PLAN = [
  W('09-Training-Plan/01-band-1500-1700.md', 'Training Roadmap: 1500 to 1700 FIDE (consolidating the basics, eliminating blunders)', 'Concrete plan: daily tactics, basic endgames (K+P, opposition, Lucena/Philidor intro), simple repertoire, blunder-checking habit, analyze every game. Milestones, time budget, what to study and skip. Include a small Mermaid roadmap flowchart for this band.'),
  W('09-Training-Plan/02-band-1700-1900.md', 'Training Roadmap: 1700 to 1900 FIDE (calculation and positional foundations)', 'Deeper tactics (combinations, calculation method), pawn structures, the bishop pair, rook endgames, repertoire understanding. Milestones and weekly plan. Include a Mermaid roadmap flowchart.'),
  W('09-Training-Plan/03-band-1900-2100.md', 'Training Roadmap: 1900 to 2100 FIDE (Candidate Master / Expert level skills)', 'Advanced strategy (prophylaxis, two weaknesses, maneuvering), complex endgames, serious opening prep with a database, model games. Milestones, weekly plan, Mermaid flowchart.'),
  W('09-Training-Plan/04-band-2100-2300.md', 'Training Roadmap: 2100 to 2300 FIDE (FIDE Master / National Master strength)', 'Deep calculation and visualization, refined repertoire with novelties, endgame mastery, opponent preparation, managing tournaments. Milestones, weekly plan, Mermaid flowchart.'),
  W('09-Training-Plan/05-band-2300-2500.md', 'Training Roadmap: 2300 to 2500 FIDE (International Master toward Grandmaster)', 'Professional-level preparation, working with engines/databases at depth, possibly a coach/trainer, norm hunting, physical and psychological prep, eliminating remaining weaknesses. Milestones, Mermaid flowchart.'),
  W('09-Training-Plan/06-band-2500-2800.md', 'Training Roadmap: 2500 to 2800 FIDE (Grandmaster to world-class)', 'What separates GMs from super-GMs: opening science and novelties, prodigious prep, peak calculation, deep endgame and prophylactic understanding, sports-science level preparation, team of seconds. Realistic note on talent/time. Mermaid flowchart.'),
  W('09-Training-Plan/weekly-schedule-templates.md', 'Weekly Study Schedule Templates (by available hours)', 'Ready-to-use weekly schedules for 5, 10 and 20 hours/week, splitting tactics, endgames, strategy, openings, play and analysis. Tables.'),
  W('09-Training-Plan/recommended-resources.md', 'Recommended Books, Courses and Tools by Rating Band', 'A curated, accurate list of well-known books and resources by topic and level (e.g. tactics: Yusupov series, Polgar; endgames: Silman, Dvoretsky; strategy: Nimzowitsch, Watson; calculation: Aagaard). Note which suit which band. Be accurate with titles/authors.'),
]

// ---------------- PSYCHOLOGY + START HERE ----------------
const PSYCH = [
  W('10-Tournament-and-Psychology/time-management-and-the-clock.md', 'Time Management and the Clock', 'Budgeting time, avoiding time trouble, when to think long vs play fast, increment/delay handling, the danger of the first quick move after long thought. Practical rules.'),
  W('10-Tournament-and-Psychology/tournament-preparation.md', 'Tournament Preparation (before and during an event)', 'Pre-event opening prep, scouting opponents, round-by-round routine, recovering between rounds, when to take a quick draw or push. Practical checklist.'),
  W('10-Tournament-and-Psychology/psychology-tilt-and-mental-game.md', 'Psychology, Tilt and the Mental Game', 'Handling losses, avoiding tilt, focus and emotional control, confidence vs overconfidence, playing the board not the opponent, dealing with nerves. Techniques.'),
  W('10-Tournament-and-Psychology/physical-fitness-and-stamina.md', 'Physical Fitness, Sleep and Stamina for Chess', 'Why elite players train physically, sleep and nutrition on game day, energy management across long games and events, the link between fitness and calculation stamina.'),
  W('00-Start-Here/how-to-use-this-guide.md', 'How to Use This Guide', 'Explain the folder structure, the rating-band roadmaps, how diagrams (ASCII + FEN + Lichess links) and flowcharts work, how to view FENs as real boards, and a suggested starting path. Reference the top-level README and ROADMAP.'),
  W('00-Start-Here/the-rating-ladder-explained.md', 'The Rating Ladder Explained (titles and what each level really means)', 'Explain FIDE rating bands and titles (CM, FM, IM, GM, the 2200/2300/2400/2500 norms idea), what skills typically separate each 200-point band, and realistic timelines. A Mermaid ladder flowchart.'),
  W('00-Start-Here/self-assessment-and-diagnostics.md', 'Self-Assessment and Diagnostics (find your weaknesses)', 'How to diagnose your weakest area (tactics vs endgames vs strategy vs time), simple self-tests, using your game stats and an error log to prioritize study. A diagnostic Mermaid flowchart.'),
]

// ---------------- FLOWCHARTS ----------------
const FLOW = [
  W('flowcharts/master-roadmap-flowchart.md', 'Master Roadmap Flowchart: 1500 to 2800', 'A single large Mermaid flowchart (flowchart TD) showing the whole journey: the rating bands as stages, the skills unlocked at each, and the feedback loop (play, analyze, train weakness). Add a short legend and links to each band roadmap file. This file is mostly the flowchart plus brief notes; the standard 11-section template does NOT apply here.'),
  W('flowcharts/the-thinking-process.md', 'The Thinking Process at the Board (move-selection flowchart)', 'A Mermaid flowchart for choosing a move every turn: opponent threats? checks/captures/threats? candidate moves? calculate forcing lines? safe? Plus a blunder-check step before moving. Brief notes. The standard 11-section template does NOT apply; focus on the flowchart.'),
  W('flowcharts/tactic-recognition.md', 'Tactic Recognition Flowchart (do I have a combination?)', 'A Mermaid flowchart: scan for loose pieces, exposed king, overloaded defenders, alignment of pieces (forks/pins/skewers), back rank; then verify with calculation. Link to each tactic file. Brief notes; flowchart-focused, not the 11-section template.'),
  W('flowcharts/endgame-decision-tree.md', 'Endgame Decision Tree (what kind of endgame is this and what is the plan?)', 'A Mermaid flowchart routing by material (K+P, R+P, minor piece, opposite bishops, Q vs P) to the right technique/file. Brief notes; flowchart-focused.'),
  W('flowcharts/opening-choice.md', 'Opening Choice Flowchart (picking what to play)', 'A Mermaid flowchart guiding repertoire choice by style (attacking vs positional, tactical vs solid, time to study). Links to opening files. Brief notes; flowchart-focused.'),
  W('flowcharts/when-to-trade-pieces.md', 'When to Trade Pieces Flowchart', 'A Mermaid flowchart: am I ahead in material? attacking? cramped? is it my good piece or bad piece? trading into a winning/drawn endgame? Brief notes; flowchart-focused.'),
]

// ===================== RUN =====================
const results = { tactics: 0, endgames: 0, other: 0 }

// Tactics: write then verify (pipeline, no barrier)
phase('Tactics')
const tac = await pipeline(
  TACTICS,
  s => agent(makePrompt(s), { label: name(s), phase: 'Tactics' }),
  (_r, s) => agent(`${VERIFY}\n\nFILE PATH: ${ROOT}${s.path}`, { label: 'verify ' + name(s), phase: 'Verify Tactics' })
)
results.tactics = tac.filter(Boolean).length
log(`Tactics done: ${results.tactics}/${TACTICS.length} verified`)

// Endgames: write then verify
phase('Endgames')
const end = await pipeline(
  ENDGAMES,
  s => agent(makePrompt(s), { label: name(s), phase: 'Endgames' }),
  (_r, s) => agent(`${VERIFY}\n\nFILE PATH: ${ROOT}${s.path}`, { label: 'verify ' + name(s), phase: 'Verify Endgames' })
)
results.endgames = end.filter(Boolean).length
log(`Endgames done: ${results.endgames}/${ENDGAMES.length} verified`)

// Conceptual groups: parallel write (lower accuracy risk)
const runGroup = (group, ph) => parallel(group.map(s => () => agent(makePrompt(s), { label: name(s), phase: ph })))

phase('Strategy')
const st = await runGroup(STRATEGY, 'Strategy')
phase('Openings')
const op = await runGroup(OPENINGS, 'Openings')
phase('Calculation')
const ca = await runGroup(CALC, 'Calculation')
phase('Mindset')
const mi = await runGroup(MINDSET, 'Mindset')
phase('Attack and Defense')
const ad = await runGroup(ATKDEF, 'Attack and Defense')
phase('Training Plan')
const pl = await runGroup(PLAN, 'Training Plan')
phase('Psychology and Start')
const ps = await runGroup(PSYCH, 'Psychology and Start')
phase('Flowcharts')
const fl = await runGroup(FLOW, 'Flowcharts')

results.other = [st, op, ca, mi, ad, pl, ps, fl].reduce((a, g) => a + g.filter(Boolean).length, 0)

// Folder index READMEs (cheap)
phase('Folder Indexes')
const idx = (folder, title, purpose, specs) => ({
  path: `${folder}/README.md`,
  prompt: `Write a concise README.md index page (GitHub-flavored Markdown) for the "${title}" section of a 1500-to-2800 FIDE chess curriculum.
Purpose of this section: ${purpose}
Include: a short H1 title, 2 to 3 sentences on what this section covers and why it matters, then a markdown table listing each file as a link with a one-line description, then a short "Suggested order" note and a line linking back to the top-level ../README.md and ../ROADMAP.md.
Files in this folder (filename -> topic):
${specs.map(s => `- ${name(s.path)} -> ${s.topic}`).join('\n')}
Save with the Write tool to EXACTLY: ${ROOT}${folder}/README.md . Reply with one short line.`,
})
const READMES = [
  idx('00-Start-Here', 'Start Here', 'orientation and how to use the guide', PSYCH.filter(s => s.path.startsWith('00'))),
  idx('01-Mindset-and-Study-Method', 'Mindset and Study Method', 'how to actually improve and study effectively', MINDSET),
  idx('02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation', 'Board Vision and Calculation', 'seeing the board and calculating accurately', CALC),
  idx('03-Tactics', 'Tactics', 'all tactical motifs from fork to combinations', TACTICS),
  idx('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play', 'Strategy and Positional Play', 'long-term positional understanding', STRATEGY),
  idx('05-Endgames', 'Endgames', 'endgame technique from K+P to rook endings', ENDGAMES),
  idx('06-Openings', 'Openings', 'opening principles and repertoire building', OPENINGS),
  idx('07-Attacking-Play', 'Attacking Play', 'attacking the king and sacrifices', ATKDEF.filter(s => s.path.startsWith('07'))),
  idx('08-Defense-and-Counterattack', 'Defense and Counterattack', 'defending and counterattacking', ATKDEF.filter(s => s.path.startsWith('08'))),
  idx('09-Training-Plan', 'Training Plan', 'rating-band roadmaps and schedules', PLAN),
  idx('10-Tournament-and-Psychology', 'Tournament and Psychology', 'competitive and mental-game skills', PSYCH.filter(s => s.path.startsWith('10'))),
]
const readmeRes = await parallel(READMES.map(r => () => agent(r.prompt, { label: 'README ' + r.path.split('/')[0], phase: 'Folder Indexes', effort: 'low' })))
results.readmes = readmeRes.filter(Boolean).length

log(`ALL DONE — tactics:${results.tactics} endgames:${results.endgames} other:${results.other} readmes:${results.readmes}`)
return results

<tool_use_error>InputValidationError: Workflow failed due to the following issue:

lastPrompt: Make a Complete File Directory containing all the road to chess grandmaster from intermediate(1500 elo  FIDE) to Complete advanced expert(2800 elo FIDE)   including various steps and complete guide ,A…

aiTitle: Chess progression guide from 1500 to 2800 Elo

content.text: Workflows run in the background automatically — I'll resend without that parameter.

input.script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'chess-grandmaster-curriculum',
  description: 'Author a complete 1500 to 2800 FIDE chess curriculum: tactics, strategy, endgames, openings, plans, with diagrams, Mermaid flowcharts and exercises; verify chess accuracy of concrete files.',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Tactics' },
    { title: 'Verify Tactics' },
    { title: 'Endgames' },
    { title: 'Verify Endgames' },
    { title: 'Strategy' },
    { title: 'Openings' },
    { title: 'Calculation' },
    { title: 'Mindset' },
    { title: 'Attack and Defense' },
    { title: 'Training Plan' },
    { title: 'Psychology and Start' },
    { title: 'Flowcharts' },
    { title: 'Folder Indexes' },
  ],
}

const ROOT = 'C:/GrandMaster/'
const name = p => p.split('/').pop()

const SG = `You are an expert chess coach and trainer (FIDE 2500+) writing ONE markdown file for a comprehensive "1500 to 2800 FIDE" study curriculum. Write GitHub-flavored Markdown, accurate, concrete and coach-like. Target 1000 to 1800 words.

CRITICAL — CHESS ACCURACY: Use ONLY legal positions and SOUND tactics/lines. Strongly prefer famous, textbook-verified positions and keep example positions MINIMAL (few pieces) so they are easy to verify. Use standard algebraic notation. Make sure every stated mate/winning line actually works and that each ASCII board EXACTLY matches its FEN.

REQUIRED STRUCTURE (use these exact section headings in order):
1. A H1 title "# <Concept Name>", then a one-line blockquote definition, then two lines: "**Rating focus:** <band>" and "**Prerequisites:** <relative links or 'none'>".
2. "## The idea" — plain-English explanation.
3. "## Why it matters" — practical value at the board.
4. "## Diagrams" — at least TWO board diagrams. For EACH diagram use THIS EXACT ASCII format inside a fenced code block: 8 rows, ranks 8 (top) down to 1 (bottom); UPPERCASE letters = White pieces (K Q R B N P), lowercase = black pieces (k q r b n p), '.' = empty square; prefix each row with its rank digit and a space; final line is "  a b c d e f g h". Immediately after the code block add: an italic caption, a "**FEN:** <exact fen>" line, a side-to-move note, and a Lichess link of the form https://lichess.org/analysis/<FEN with every space replaced by an underscore>. Then explain the key moves in algebraic notation.
5. "## Patterns and sub-types" — bullet the main variations and related motifs.
6. "## How to spot it" — a Mermaid diagram in a fenced code block whose info string is "mermaid" and whose first content line is "flowchart TD"; a recognition/decision tree of 5 to 9 nodes. Node text in square brackets, arrows use -->, edge labels use |label|. Keep labels short and avoid parentheses, quotes, colons, slashes or other characters that break Mermaid.
7. "## Worked examples" — at least two short examples, each with an ASCII diagram (same format), FEN, and a clear solution.
8. "## Common mistakes" — bullets.
9. "## Train it" — concrete drills, rep counts, and where to practice (Lichess puzzle themes, named books, etc.).
10. "## Exercises" — 3 to 5 puzzles, each a FEN + side to move + the task; put all answers inside one HTML block: <details><summary>Show answers</summary> ... </details>.
11. "## Related" — relative markdown links to sibling files like [Deflection](deflection.md), plus double-bracket concept tags like [[deflection]].

Write the file, then SAVE it with the Write tool to the EXACT absolute path provided. After saving, reply with ONE short line: the path, the word count, and the FENs you used.`

const VERIFY = `You are a meticulous chess fact-checker (FIDE 2400+). Read the markdown file at the given absolute path, then verify EVERY ASCII board diagram, FEN and analysis line:
- Each ASCII board must EXACTLY match its FEN (piece types and exact files/ranks; UPPERCASE=White, lowercase=black, '.'=empty; rank 8 on top, rank 1 at bottom; 8 cells per row).
- Each FEN must be legal: exactly one king per side, no pawns on the 1st or 8th rank, a plausible legal position.
- Each stated tactic/mate/winning line must be SOUND. If a line is unsound or a position is illegal, FIX it: replace with a correct minimal position illustrating the SAME theme and update the ASCII board, FEN, Lichess link and analysis to match.
- Mermaid blocks must parse: first content line "flowchart TD", arrows -->, no parentheses/quotes/colons inside node labels.
- Notation must be correct algebraic.
Make fixes IN PLACE with the Edit tool. Do not rewrite good content or change structure. Reply with a short bullet list of fixes made, or "No changes needed".`

const makePrompt = s => `${SG}

TOPIC FOR THIS FILE: ${s.topic}
GUIDANCE / POSITIONS TO USE: ${s.hints}
EXACT SAVE PATH (use Write to this absolute path): ${ROOT}${s.path}`

const W = (path, topic, hints) => ({ path, topic, hints })

const TACTICS = [
  W('03-Tactics/fork.md', 'The Fork (one piece attacking two or more enemy targets at once)', 'Cover knight forks including the royal fork on king+queen, the family fork (K+Q+R), pawn forks, bishop/rook/queen forks. Use a minimal position of a white knight forking the black king and rook, and a pawn forking two pieces.'),
  W('03-Tactics/pin.md', 'The Pin (a piece cannot or should not move because a more valuable piece sits behind it)', 'Distinguish ABSOLUTE pin (against the king, illegal to move) vs RELATIVE pin. Show a bishop pinning a knight to the king, and piling up on the pinned piece / pin-to-win. Mention not to leave pieces pinned.'),
  W('03-Tactics/skewer.md', 'The Skewer (the reverse pin: a valuable piece is attacked and forced to move, exposing a lesser piece behind it)', 'Use a rook or bishop skewering the black king and queen on a line, winning the queen. Show a minimal R+K vs K+Q skewer and a bishop skewer on a diagonal.'),
  W('03-Tactics/discovered-attack.md', 'The Discovered Attack (moving one piece unveils an attack from a piece behind it)', 'Show a discovered attack where moving a knight or bishop unveils a rook or queen attack, often with the moving piece ALSO making a threat. Keep minimal.'),
  W('03-Tactics/discovered-check.md', 'Discovered Check and Double Check', 'Explain discovered check and DOUBLE CHECK (both pieces give check, king MUST move). Note double check is the most forcing move. Link to windmill and smothered mate.'),
  W('03-Tactics/double-attack.md', 'The Double Attack (creating two simultaneous threats so the opponent cannot meet both)', 'Broader than the fork: a queen attacking two loose pieces, a check that also attacks a piece, threats of mate plus material.   W('03-Tactics/decoy.md', 'The Decoy (attraction: luring an enemy piece, often the king, to a bad square via a sacrifice)', 'Classic: sacrifice to drag the king onto a square where a knight fork or check wins the queen.   W('03-Tactics/deflection.md', 'Deflection (luring or forcing a defender away from a key duty)', 'Show a back-rank deflection: deflect the rook or queen guarding the back rank, then mate. Also deflection that removes the guard of a key square. Contrast with decoy and removing-the-defender.'),
  W('03-Tactics/removing-the-defender.md', 'Removing the Defender (eliminating the piece that guards a key square, piece or mating square)', 'Capture or chase the defender, then execute the threat. Show capturing a knight that defends a back-rank mating square.'),
  W('03-Tactics/overloading.md', 'The Overloaded Piece (a defender tasked with too many duties)', 'A piece guarding two things at once; attack one duty and the other collapses. Minimal example with one rook defending both a back-rank square and a hanging piece.'),
  W('03-Tactics/interference.md', 'Interference and Obstruction (interposing a piece to cut the line between a defender and what it defends)', 'Block the defensive line, often with a sacrifice, so a piece is no longer guarded. Classic interference motif.'),

  W('03-Tactics/zwischenzug.md', 'Zwischenzug / Intermezzo (an in-between move inserted before the expected recapture)', 'Insert a more forcing move, a check or bigger threat, before recapturing, changing the outcome. Minimal example where a check wins a tempo or piece before recapture.'),
  W('03-Tactics/x-ray.md', 'The X-ray (a long-range piece acts through an enemy or friendly piece on a line)', 'X-ray attack and X-ray defense: a rook, queen or bishop defends or attacks a square THROUGH an intervening piece. Distinguish from skewer.'),
  W('03-Tactics/windmill.md', 'The Windmill / See-saw (a repeating discovered-check mechanism that harvests material)', 'Explain with the famous Carlos Torre vs Emanuel Lasker, Moscow 1925 windmill, rook plus bishop see-saw on the 7th rank. const results = { tactics: 0, endgames: 0, other: 0, readmes: 0 }

phase('Tactics')
const tac = await pipeline(
  TACTICS,
  s => agent(makePrompt(s), { label: name(s), phase: 'Tactics' }),
  (_r, s) => agent(`${VERIFY}\n\nFILE PATH: ${ROOT}${s.path}`, { label: 'verify ' + name(s), phase: 'Verify Tactics' })
)
results.tactics = tac.filter(Boolean).length
log(`Tactics done: ${results.tactics}/${TACTICS.length}`)

phase('Endgames')
const end = await pipeline(
  ENDGAMES,
  s => agent(makePrompt(s), { label: name(s), phase: 'Endgames' }),
  (_r, s) => agent(`${VERIFY}\n\nFILE PATH: ${ROOT}${s.path}`, { label: 'verify ' + name(s), phase: 'Verify Endgames' })
)
results.endgames = end.filter(Boolean).length
log(`Endgames done: ${results.endgames}/${ENDGAMES.length}`)

const runGroup = (group, ph) => parallel(group.map(s => () => agent(makePrompt(s), { label: name(s), phase: ph })))

phase('Strategy')
const st = await runGroup(STRATEGY, 'Strategy')
phase('Openings')
const op = await runGroup(OPENINGS, 'Openings')
phase('Calculation')
const ca = await runGroup(CALC, 'Calculation')
phase('Mindset')
const mi = await runGroup(MINDSET, 'Mindset')
phase('Attack and Defense')
const ad = await runGroup(ATKDEF, 'Attack and Defense')
phase('Training Plan')
const pl = await runGroup(PLAN, 'Training Plan')
phase('Psychology and Start')
const ps = await runGroup(PSYCH, 'Psychology and Start')
phase('Flowcharts')
const fl = await runGroup(FLOW, 'Flowcharts')
results.other = [st, op, ca, mi, ad, pl, ps, fl].reduce((a, g) => a + g.filter(Boolean).length, 0)

phase('Folder Indexes')
const idx = (folder, title, purpose, specs) => ({
  path: `${folder}/README.md`,
  prompt: `Write a concise README.md index page (GitHub-flavored Markdown) for the "${title}" section of a 1500-to-2800 FIDE chess curriculum.
Purpose of this section: ${purpose}
Include: a short H1 title, 2 to 3 sentences on what this section covers and why it matters, then a markdown table listing each file as a link with a one-line description, then a short "Suggested order" note and a line linking back to the top-level ../README.md and ../ROADMAP.md.
Files in this folder (filename then topic):
${specs.map(s => `- ${name(s.path)} : ${s.topic}`).join('\n')}
Save with the Write tool to EXACTLY: ${ROOT}${folder}/README.md . Reply with one short line.`,
})
const READMES = [
  idx('00-Start-Here', 'Start Here', 'orientation and how to use the guide', PSYCH.filter(s => s.path.startsWith('00'))),
  idx('01-Mindset-and-Study-Method', 'Mindset and Study Method', 'how to actually improve and study effectively', MINDSET),
  idx('02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation', 'Board Vision and Calculation', 'seeing the board and calculating accurately', CALC),
  idx('03-Tactics', 'Tactics', 'all tactical motifs from fork to combinations', TACTICS),
  idx('04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play', 'Strategy and Positional Play', 'long-term positional understanding', STRATEGY),
  idx('05-Endgames', 'Endgames', 'endgame technique from K+P to rook endings', ENDGAMES),
  idx('06-Openings', 'Openings', 'opening principles and repertoire building', OPENINGS),
  idx('07-Attacking-Play', 'Attacking Play', 'attacking the king and sacrifices', ATKDEF.filter(s => s.path.startsWith('07'))),
  idx('08-Defense-and-Counterattack', 'Defense and Counterattack', 'defending and counterattacking', ATKDEF.filter(s => s.path.startsWith('08'))),
  idx('09-Training-Plan', 'Training Plan', 'rating-band roadmaps and schedules', PLAN),
  idx('10-Tournament-and-Psychology', 'Tournament and Psychology', 'competitive and mental-game skills', PSYCH.filter(s => s.path.startsWith('10'))),
]
const readmeRes = await parallel(READMES.map(r => () => agent(r.prompt, { label: 'README ' + r.path.split('/')[0], phase: 'Folder Indexes', effort: 'low' })))
results.readmes = readmeRes.filter(Boolean).length

log(`ALL DONE — tactics:${results.tactics} endgames:${results.endgames} other:${results.other} readmes:${results.readmes}`)
return results

input.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
render_board.py — a tiny, dependency-free FEN -> SVG chessboard renderer.

Why this exists:
    The whole curriculum stores positions as FEN strings plus ASCII boards so it
    is 100% portable and never breaks. This tool turns ANY FEN into a real, sharp
    SVG image (which renders as a picture in browsers, VS Code, Obsidian, GitHub,
    etc.) so you can illustrate any position you like.

Usage:
    python tools/render_board.py "<FEN>" <output.svg> [--caption "text"] [--flip] [--size 64]

    # Only the board (placement) field of the FEN is used, so a full FEN or just
    # the placement field both work:
    python tools/render_board.py "r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3" assets/knight-fork.svg --caption "Nc7+ forks king and rook"

    # Generate the whole showcase gallery used by the guide:
    python tools/render_board.py --gallery assets

No third-party packages required (pure standard library). Python 3.8+.
"""

import argparse
import os
import sys

# Solid Unicode chess glyphs are used for BOTH colours; colour is conveyed by the
# SVG fill/stroke instead of by the glyph, which gives clear contrast on light and
# dark squares alike.
GLYPH = {
    "k": "♚", "q": "♛", "r": "♜",
    "b": "♝", "n": "♞", "p": "♟",
}

LIGHT = "#f0d9b5"   # classic lichess "brown" light square
DARK = "#b58863"    # classic lichess "brown" dark square
WHITE_FILL = "#ffffff"
WHITE_STROKE = "#3a3a3a"
BLACK_FILL = "#2b2b2b"
BLACK_STROKE = "#101010"
LABEL = "#5b4636"

FILES = "abcdefgh"


def parse_placement(fen: str):
    """Return an 8x8 grid (rank 8 first) of piece chars or None."""
    placement = fen.strip().split()[0]
    rows = placement.split("/")
    if len(rows) != 8:
        raise ValueError(f"FEN placement must have 8 ranks, got {len(rows)}: {placement!r}")
    grid = []
    for r in rows:
        row = []
        for ch in r:
            if ch.isdigit():
                row.extend([None] * int(ch))
            else:
                row.append(ch)
        if len(row) != 8:
            raise ValueError(f"Rank {r!r} does not describe 8 squares")
        grid.append(row)
    return grid


def render(fen: str, caption: str = "", flip: bool = False, sq: int = 64) -> str:
    grid = parse_placement(fen)
    if flip:
        grid = [list(reversed(r)) for r in reversed(grid)]

    margin = int(sq * 0.45)          # room for coordinates
    cap_h = int(sq * 0.55) if caption else 0
    board = sq * 8
    w = board + margin * 2
    h = board + margin * 2 + cap_h
    glyph_size = int(sq * 0.78)
    label_size = int(sq * 0.26)

    out = []
    out.append(
        f'<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="{w}" height="{h}" '
        f'viewBox="0 0 {w} {h}" role="img" aria-label="chess position {fen}">'
    )
    out.append(f'<rect width="{w}" height="{h}" fill="#faf3e0"/>')

    font = ("font-family=\"'Segoe UI Symbol','Noto Sans Symbols2','DejaVu Sans',"
            "'Arial Unicode MS',sans-serif\"")

    for rank in range(8):       # 0 = top row (rank 8 or 1 if flipped)
        for file in range(8):
            x = margin + file * sq
            y = margin + rank * sq
            is_light = (rank + file) % 2 == 0
            color = LIGHT if is_light else DARK
            out.append(f'<rect x="{x}" y="{y}" width="{sq}" height="{sq}" fill="{color}"/>')
            piece = grid[rank][file]
            if piece:
                glyph = GLYPH[piece.lower()]
                cx = x + sq / 2
                cy = y + sq / 2 + glyph_size * 0.34
                if piece.isupper():     # White
                    fill, stroke = WHITE_FILL, WHITE_STROKE
                else:                   # black
                    fill, stroke = BLACK_FILL, BLACK_STROKE
                out.append(
                    f'<text x="{cx:.1f}" y="{cy:.1f}" font-size="{glyph_size}" '
                    f'text-anchor="middle" {font} fill="{fill}" stroke="{stroke}" '
                    f'stroke-width="{max(1, sq//40)}" paint-order="stroke">{glyph}</text>'
                )

    # Coordinates
    rank_labels = list(range(8, 0, -1)) if not flip else list(range(1, 9))
    file_labels = list(FILES) if not flip else list(reversed(FILES))
    for i, rl in enumerate(rank_labels):
        y = margin + i * sq + sq / 2 + label_size * 0.35
        out.append(
            f'<text x="{margin*0.55:.1f}" y="{y:.1f}" font-size="{label_size}" '
            f'text-anchor="middle" {font} fill="{LABEL}">{rl}</text>'
        )
    for i, fl in enumerate(file_labels):
        x = margin + i * sq + sq / 2
        y = margin + board + margin * 0.62
        out.append(
            f'<text x="{x:.1f}" y="{y:.1f}" font-size="{label_size}" '
            f'text-anchor="middle" {font} fill="{LABEL}">{fl}</text>'
        )

    if caption:
        safe = (caption.replace("&", "&").replace("<", "<").replace(">", ">"))
        out.append(
            f'<text x="{w/2:.1f}" y="{margin + board + margin + cap_h*0.55:.1f}" '
            f'font-size="{int(sq*0.26)}" text-anchor="middle" '
            f'font-family="Segoe UI,Helvetica,Arial,sans-serif" fill="#3a2e22">{safe}</text>'
        )

    out.append("</svg>")
    return "\n".join(out)


# Verified, minimal showcase positions used across the guide.
GALLERY = [
    ("start.svg", "rnbqkbnr/pppppppp/8/8/8/8/PPPPPPPP/RNBQKBNR", "Starting position"),
    ("knight-fork.svg", "r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3", "Knight on c7 forks the black king and rook"),
    ("pin.svg", "3k4/8/5n2/6B1/8/8/8/4K3", "Bg5 pins the f6-knight to the king on d8"),
    ("skewer.svg", "q7/8/8/8/k7/8/8/R3K3", "Ra1+ skewers the king; then Rxa8 wins the queen"),
    ("back-rank-mate.svg", "4R1k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/8/6K1", "Back-rank mate: Re8 is checkmate"),
    ("smothered-mate.svg", "6rk/5Npp/8/8/8/8/8/6K1", "Smothered mate: Nf7 is checkmate"),
    ("opposition.svg", "8/3k4/8/3K4/8/8/8/8", "Direct opposition on the d-file"),
    ("greek-gift.svg", "r1bq1rk1/pppn1ppp/3b4/3pp3/3P4/2NBPN2/PPP2PPP/R1BQ1RK1", "Greek Gift setup: White eyes Bxh7+"),
]


def write_gallery(outdir: str):
    os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True)
    for fname, fen, cap in GALLERY:
        svg = render(fen, caption=cap)
        path = os.path.join(outdir, fname)
        with open(path, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write(svg)
        print(f"wrote {path}")


def main():
    ap = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Render a FEN to an SVG chessboard.")
    ap.add_argument("fen", nargs="?", help="FEN string (full or placement field only)")
    ap.add_argument("output", nargs="?", help="output .svg path")
    ap.add_argument("--caption", default="", help="caption text under the board")
    ap.add_argument("--flip", action="store_true", help="view from Black's side")
    ap.add_argument("--size", type=int, default=64, help="square size in px (default 64)")
    ap.add_argument("--gallery", metavar="DIR", help="generate the showcase gallery into DIR")
    args = ap.parse_args()

    if args.gallery:
        write_gallery(args.gallery)
        return
    if not args.fen or not args.output:
        ap.error("provide FEN and output path, or use --gallery DIR")
    svg = render(args.fen, caption=args.caption, flip=args.flip, sq=args.size)
    with open(args.output, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write(svg)
    print(f"wrote {args.output}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

input.content:
# ♟️ The Road to Grandmaster — A Complete Chess Curriculum (1500 → 2800 FIDE)

> A full, self-contained training library that takes you from a **1500 FIDE intermediate** to a **2800 FIDE world-class expert**. Every tactical motif, every positional concept, every essential endgame — explained from scratch, with board diagrams, FEN strings, decision **flowcharts**, generated **images**, and graded exercises.

This is not a book you read once. It is a **map + toolkit + training plan** you work through, return to, and train against for years.

---

## 🚀 Start here (3 steps)

1. **Read [`ROADMAP.md`](ROADMAP.md)** — the big-picture journey, the master flowchart, and what each rating band requires.
2. **Read [`00-Start-Here/how-to-use-this-guide.md`](00-Start-Here/how-to-use-this-guide.md)** and take the [self-assessment](00-Start-Here/self-assessment-and-diagnostics.md) to find your weakest area.
3. **Open your rating band's roadmap** in [`09-Training-Plan/`](09-Training-Plan/) and follow it. Train tactics daily, study one concept file at a time, and **analyze every serious game you play**.

---

## 🗂️ Directory map

```
GrandMaster/
├── README.md ................ you are here (master index)
├── ROADMAP.md ............... the 1500→2800 journey + master flowchart
├── GLOSSARY.md ............. every term defined, with links
│
├── 00-Start-Here/ .......... orientation, the rating ladder, self-diagnosis
├── 01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/ .. how to actually improve (deliberate practice, analysis)
├── 02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/ .. visualization, candidate moves, evaluation
│
├── 03-Tactics/ ............. ★ every motif: fork, pin, skewer, decoy,
│                              deflection, double attack, discovered attack,
│                              zwischenzug, x-ray, windmill, back-rank,
│                              smothered mate, combinations, mating patterns …
│
├── 04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/ .. pawn structures, outposts, files,
│                              bishop pair, good/bad bishop, prophylaxis,
│                              two weaknesses, king safety …
│
├── 05-Endgames/ ............ ★ K+P endings, the opposition, TRIANGULATION,
│                              zugzwang, key squares, Lucena & Philidor,
│                              rook/queen vs pawn, opposite bishops, fortress …
│
├── 06-Openings/ ............ principles, repertoire building, 1.e4 e5,
│                              Sicilian/French/Caro, Queen's Gambit, Indians, gambits
│
├── 07-Attacking-Play/ ...... attacking the castled king, the Greek Gift,
│                              fianchetto sacrifices, pawn storms, the initiative
│
├── 08-Defense-and-Counterattack/ .. defense principles, counterattack, saving lost games
│
├── 09-Training-Plan/ ....... ★ a roadmap for EACH rating band (1500-1700 …
│                              2500-2800), weekly schedules, recommended books
│
├── 10-Tournament-and-Psychology/ .. time management, prep, tilt, fitness
│
├── flowcharts/ ............. the decision diagrams (thinking process, tactic
│                              recognition, endgame tree, master roadmap …)
├── assets/ ................. generated SVG board images + gallery
└── tools/ .................. render_board.py — turn ANY FEN into an image
```

Each folder has its own **`README.md` index**. Each concept file follows the same structure: *the idea → why it matters → diagrams → sub-types → a recognition flowchart → worked examples → common mistakes → how to train it → exercises with answers → related links.*

---

## 🧭 How to read the diagrams (important)

Positions are shown **three ways** so they work everywhere and never break:

**1. An ASCII board** (uppercase = White, lowercase = Black, `.` = empty):

```
8 r . . . k . . .
7 . . N . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . K . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```

**2. A FEN string** you can paste into any board — e.g. **FEN:** `r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3 b - - 0 1`

**3. A Lichess link** that opens the live, interactive board:
`https://lichess.org/analysis/r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3_b_-_-_0_1` *(spaces in the FEN become `_`)*.

### Want real images?

The showcase positions are pre-rendered as SVG in [`assets/`](assets/) (see the **[visual gallery](assets/gallery.md)**). To turn **any** FEN into an image yourself:

```bash
python tools/render_board.py "r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3" my-board.svg --caption "Knight fork"
# regenerate the whole gallery:
python tools/render_board.py --gallery assets
```

---

## 🖼️ A few showcase positions

| The Fork | The Pin | The Skewer |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ![Knight fork](assets/knight-fork.svg) | ![Pin](assets/pin.svg) | ![Skewer](assets/skewer.svg) |
| [Back-rank mate](03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md) | [Smothered mate](03-Tactics/smothered-mate.md) | [The opposition](05-Endgames/the-opposition.md) |
| ![Back rank](assets/back-rank-mate.svg) | ![Smothered](assets/smothered-mate.svg) | ![Opposition](assets/opposition.svg) |

> If the SVGs do not display in your viewer, open the [gallery](assets/gallery.md), or just use the FEN/Lichess links inside each file. Mermaid **flowcharts** render on GitHub and in VS Code (with the Markdown Preview Mermaid extension) and Obsidian.

---

## 📚 The concept index (the things you asked to learn)

**Tactics** ([folder](03-Tactics/)): [Fork](03-Tactics/fork.md) · [Pin](03-Tactics/pin.md) · [Skewer](03-Tactics/skewer.md) · [Discovered attack](03-Tactics/discovered-attack.md) · [Discovered & double check](03-Tactics/discovered-check.md) · [Double attack](03-Tactics/double-attack.md) · [Decoy](03-Tactics/decoy.md) · [Deflection](03-Tactics/deflection.md) · [Removing the defender](03-Tactics/removing-the-defender.md) · [Overloading](03-Tactics/overloading.md) · [Interference](03-Tactics/interference.md) · [Clearance sacrifice](03-Tactics/clearance-sacrifice.md) · [Zwischenzug](03-Tactics/zwischenzug.md) · [X-ray](03-Tactics/x-ray.md) · [Windmill](03-Tactics/windmill.md) · [Trapped piece](03-Tactics/trapped-piece.md) · [Back-rank mate](03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md) · [Smothered mate](03-Tactics/smothered-mate.md) · [Desperado](03-Tactics/desperado.md) · [Underpromotion](03-Tactics/underpromotion.md) · [Combinations](03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md) · [Mating patterns](03-Tactics/mating-patterns.md)

**Endgames** ([folder](05-Endgames/)): [King & pawn](05-Endgames/king-and-pawn-endgames.md) · [The opposition](05-Endgames/the-opposition.md) · [**Triangulation**](05-Endgames/triangulation.md) · [Zugzwang](05-Endgames/zugzwang.md) · [Key squares](05-Endgames/key-squares-and-rule-of-the-square.md) · [Rook endings (Lucena/Philidor)](05-Endgames/rook-endgames.md) · [Rook vs pawn](05-Endgames/rook-vs-pawn.md) · [Queen vs pawn](05-Endgames/queen-vs-pawn.md) · [Minor-piece endings](05-Endgames/minor-piece-endgames.md) · [Opposite bishops](05-Endgames/opposite-colored-bishops.md) · [Fortress](05-Endgames/the-fortress.md) · [Converting](05-Endgames/converting-advantages.md) · [Breakthrough](05-Endgames/pawn-breakthrough.md)

**Strategy** ([folder](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/)), **Openings** ([folder](06-Openings/)), **Attack** ([folder](07-Attacking-Play/)), **Defense** ([folder](08-Defense-and-Counterattack/)) — see each folder's README.

---

## ✅ The one habit that matters most

Whatever your rating: **play long games, then analyze them yourself before turning on the engine.** Everything else in this guide is fuel for that loop. See [analyzing your own games](01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/analyzing-your-own-games.md).

*Built as a structured, reusable study system. Work through it section by section — and enjoy the climb.*
