**1. An ASCII board** (uppercase = White, lowercase = Black, `.` = empty):

```
8 r . . . k . . .
7 . . N . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . K . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```

**2. A FEN string** you can paste into any board — e.g. **FEN:** `r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3 b - - 0 1`

**3. A Lichess link** that opens the live, interactive board:
`https://lichess.org/analysis/r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3_b_-_-_0_1` *(spaces in the FEN become `_`)*.

### Want real images?

The showcase positions are pre-rendered as SVG in [`assets/`](assets/) (see the **[visual gallery](assets/gallery.md)**). To turn **any** FEN into an image yourself:

```bash
python tools/render_board.py "r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3" my-board.svg --caption "Knight fork"
# regenerate the whole gallery:
python tools/render_board.py --gallery assets
```

---

## 🖼️ A few showcase positions

| The Fork | The Pin | The Skewer |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| ![Knight fork](assets/knight-fork.svg) | ![Pin](assets/pin.svg) | ![Skewer](assets/skewer.svg) |
| [Back-rank mate](03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md) | [Smothered mate](03-Tactics/smothered-mate.md) | [The opposition](05-Endgames/the-opposition.md) |
| ![Back rank](assets/back-rank-mate.svg) | ![Smothered](assets/smothered-mate.svg) | ![Opposition](assets/opposition.svg) |

> If the SVGs do not display in your viewer, open the [gallery](assets/gallery.md), or just use the FEN/Lichess links inside each file. Mermaid **flowcharts** render on GitHub and in VS Code (with the Markdown Preview Mermaid extension) and Obsidian.

---

## 📚 The concept index (the things you asked to learn)

**Tactics** ([folder](03-Tactics/)): [Fork](03-Tactics/fork.md) · [Pin](03-Tactics/pin.md) · [Skewer](03-Tactics/skewer.md) · [Discovered attack](03-Tactics/discovered-attack.md) · [Discovered & double check](03-Tactics/discovered-check.md) · [Double attack](03-Tactics/double-attack.md) · [Decoy](03-Tactics/decoy.md) · [Deflection](03-Tactics/deflection.md) · [Removing the defender](03-Tactics/removing-the-defender.md) · [Overloading](03-Tactics/overloading.md) · [Interference](03-Tactics/interference.md) · [Clearance sacrifice](03-Tactics/clearance-sacrifice.md) · [Zwischenzug](03-Tactics/zwischenzug.md) · [X-ray](03-Tactics/x-ray.md) · [Windmill](03-Tactics/windmill.md) · [Trapped piece](03-Tactics/trapped-piece.md) · [Back-rank mate](03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md) · [Smothered mate](03-Tactics/smothered-mate.md) · [Desperado](03-Tactics/desperado.md) · [Underpromotion](03-Tactics/underpromotion.md) · [Combinations](03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md) · [Mating patterns](03-Tactics/mating-patterns.md)

**Endgames** ([folder](05-Endgames/)): [King & pawn](05-Endgames/king-and-pawn-endgames.md) · [The opposition](05-Endgames/the-opposition.md) · [**Triangulation**](05-Endgames/triangulation.md) · [Zugzwang](05-Endgames/zugzwang.md) · [Key squares](05-Endgames/key-squares-and-rule-of-the-square.md) · [Rook endings (Lucena/Philidor)](05-Endgames/rook-endgames.md) · [Rook vs pawn](05-Endgames/rook-vs-pawn.md) · [Queen vs pawn](05-Endgames/queen-vs-pawn.md) · [Minor-piece endings](05-Endgames/minor-piece-endgames.md) · [Opposite bishops](05-Endgames/opposite-colored-bishops.md) · [Fortress](05-Endgames/the-fortress.md) · [Converting](05-Endgames/converting-advantages.md) · [Breakthrough](05-Endgames/pawn-breakthrough.md)

**Strategy** ([folder](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/)), **Openings** ([folder](06-Openings/)), **Attack** ([folder](07-Attacking-Play/)), **Defense** ([folder](08-Defense-and-Counterattack/)) — see each folder's README.

---

## ✅ The one habit that matters most

Whatever your rating: **play long games, then analyze them yourself before turning on the engine.** Everything else in this guide is fuel for that loop. See [analyzing your own games](01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/analyzing-your-own-games.md).

*Built as a structured, reusable study system. Work through it section by section — and enjoy the climb.*

# 🧭 The Complete Roadmap: 1500 → 2800 FIDE > The whole journey on one page. What to master at each step, in what order, and the engine that drives all improvement. For the day-to-day plan of any band, open its file in [`09-Training-Plan/`](09-Training-Plan/). --- ## The master flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S([You are here ~1500 FIDE]) --> B1 B1[1500-1700 Consolidate
Stop one-move blunders
Core tactics + basic endgames] B2[1700-1900 Build
Calculation method
Pawn structures + rook endings] B3[1900-2100 Expert / CM
Prophylaxis + planning
Complex endgames] B4[2100-2300 FM / NM
Deep calculation
Real opening prep + databases] B5[2300-2500 IM toward GM
Professional preparation
Norm hunting] B6[2500-2800 GM to world class
Opening science + novelties
Peak everything] G([Grandmaster and beyond]) B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> G subgraph ENGINE[The improvement engine - repeat forever at every level] direction LR P[Play serious slow games] --> A[Analyze yourself first] A --> W[Find the single biggest weakness] W --> T[Train that weakness deliberately] T --> P end B1 -.powered by.-> ENGINE B3 -.powered by.-> ENGINE B6 -.powered by.-> ENGINE classDef band fill:#eef4ff,stroke:#3b6cb7,stroke-width:1px,color:#13294b; classDef cap fill:#fff6e6,stroke:#b5832a,color:#5b4112; class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6 band; class S,G cap; ``` > Mermaid renders on GitHub, in VS Code (Markdown Preview Mermaid Support), and Obsidian. If it shows as code in your viewer, install a Mermaid plugin or read the band table below. --- ## The bands at a glance | Band | Title milestone | The leap that gets you here | Primary focus | Roadmap | |---|---|---|---|---| | **1500–1700** | strong club player | You **stop hanging pieces** and missing one-movers | Tactics volume, board vision, basic endgames, blunder-check | [→](09-Training-Plan/01-band-1500-1700.md) | | **1700–1900** | Class A / expert track | You **calculate** instead of guessing; you understand structures | Calculation method, pawn structures, rook endgames, repertoire ideas | [→](09-Training-Plan/02-band-1700-1900.md) | | **1900–2100** | Expert / Candidate Master | You **plan** and play **prophylactically** | Strategy depth, complex endgames, database-driven prep, model games | [→](09-Training-Plan/03-band-1900-2100.md) | | **2100–2300** | FIDE Master / National Master | Your **calculation is deep and reliable**; few weaknesses remain | Visualization, refined repertoire + novelties, endgame mastery | [→](09-Training-Plan/04-band-2100-2300.md) | | **2300–2500** | International Master → GM | You **prepare like a professional** and convert technically | Pro-level prep, engine/database depth, norms, fitness & psychology | [→](09-Training-Plan/05-band-2300-2500.md) | | **2500–2800** | Grandmaster → world class | **Opening science, prodigious memory, peak calculation** | Novelties, deep theory, seconds, sports-science preparation | [→](09-Training-Plan/06-band-2500-2800.md) | --- ## What unlocks at each step (the skill tree) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FOUND[Foundations 1500-1900] F1[Tactics: fork pin skewer
discovered + double attack] F2[Endgames: opposition
key squares Lucena Philidor] F3[Calculation: checks captures threats
candidate moves] end subgraph MID[Mastery 1900-2300] M1[Strategy: structures outposts
good vs bad bishop] M2[Prophylaxis + two weaknesses] M3[Endgames: triangulation zugzwang
opposite bishops conversion] end subgraph ELITE[Elite 2300-2800] E1[Opening science + novelties] E2[Deep visualization + defense] E3[Sports-science preparation] end FOUND --> MID --> ELITE ``` You do not finish a topic and discard it. You **layer**: a 2500 still solves tactics daily — they just solve harder ones, faster. The pyramid below is the proportion of study time by level. ``` 2500-2800 [ Openings | Prep | Calc | Endgames | Fitness ] 2100-2300 [ Calculation | Strategy | Openings | Endgames ] 1700-2100 [ Tactics | Calculation | Strategy | Endgames ] 1500-1700 [ ===== Tactics ===== | Endgames | Blunder-check ] FOUNDATION [ Don't hang pieces. See the whole board. Count material. ] ``` --- ## The non-negotiables (true at every rating) 1. **Tactics daily.** Pattern recognition is the engine of results below 2200 and never stops mattering. → [`03-Tactics/`](03-Tactics/) 2. **Analyze your own games — yourself, first.** The fastest feedback loop in chess. → [analysis method](01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/analyzing-your-own-games.md) 3. **Endgame technique.** Knowing the [opposition](05-Endgames/the-opposition.md), [triangulation](05-Endgames/triangulation.md), [Lucena & Philidor](05-Endgames/rook-endgames.md) turns half-points into points. 4. **A blunder-check before every move.** → [the thinking process](flowcharts/the-thinking-process.md). 5. **Deliberate practice over volume.** Work at the edge of your ability with feedback. → [deliberate practice](01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/deliberate-practice.md). --- ## A realistic word on timelines Each 200-point band typically takes **hundreds to thousands of hours** of focused work, and the bands get **exponentially** harder — going 2500→2700 is a different universe of effort than 1500→1700, and reaching the very top also requires talent, youth, coaching and time that not everyone has. This guide gives you the **correct map and methods**; how far you travel depends on the work you put in. The good news: the methods that make a 1500 into a 2000 are the same *kind* of methods that make a 2300 into a GM — just applied deeper. Climb one band at a time. > Next: pick your band in [`09-Training-Plan/`](09-Training-Plan/), or run the [self-assessment](00-Start-Here/self-assessment-and-diagnostics.md) first.

# 🖼️ Visual Gallery

Pre-rendered board images for the showcase positions, generated by [`../tools/render_board.py`](../tools/render_board.py). Each is a real SVG image (renders as a picture in browsers, VS Code, Obsidian, GitHub). Regenerate them any time with:

```bash
python tools/render_board.py --gallery assets
```

To render **any** position you like:

```bash
python tools/render_board.py "<FEN>" assets/my-position.svg --caption "My note"
```

---

## Starting position
![Starting position](start.svg)
**FEN:** `rnbqkbnr/pppppppp/8/8/8/8/PPPPPPPP/RNBQKBNR w KQkq - 0 1`

## The Fork — knight forks king and rook
![Knight fork](knight-fork.svg)
**FEN:** `r3k3/2N5/8/8/8/8/8/4K3 b - - 0 1` — Black to move, king in check. The king must step aside and White plays **Nxa8**, winning the rook. → [Fork](../03-Tactics/fork.md)

## The Pin — bishop pins the knight to the king
![Pin](pin.svg)
**FEN:** `3k4/8/5n2/6B1/8/8/8/4K3 w - - 0 1` — The f6-knight is pinned to the king on d8 along the g5–d8 diagonal and cannot legally move. → [Pin](../03-Tactics/pin.md)

## The Skewer — the king is forced to move, the queen falls
![Skewer](skewer.svg)
**FEN:** `q7/8/8/8/k7/8/8/R3K3 b - - 0 1` — After **Ra1+**, the king must leave the a-file, then **Rxa8** wins the queen. → [Skewer](../03-Tactics/skewer.md)

## Back-rank mate
![Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.svg)
**FEN:** `4R1k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/8/6K1 b - - 0 1` — **Re8** is checkmate: the king is trapped behind its own f7/g7/h7 pawns. → [Back-rank mate](../03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md)

## Smothered mate
![Smothered mate](smothered-mate.svg)
**FEN:** `6rk/5Npp/8/8/8/8/8/6K1 b - - 0 1` — **Nf7** is checkmate: the king is hemmed in by its own rook and pawns. → [Smothered mate](../03-Tactics/smothered-mate.md)

## The Opposition
![Opposition](opposition.svg)
**FEN:** `8/3k4/8/3K4/8/8/8/8 w - - 0 1` — Kings face off with one square between them. The side **not** to move "has the opposition" and controls the key squares. → [The opposition](../05-Endgames/the-opposition.md)

## The Greek Gift setup
![Greek Gift](greek-gift.svg)
**FEN:** `r1bq1rk1/pppn1ppp/3b4/3pp3/3P4/2NBPN2/PPP2PPP/R1BQ1RK1 w - - 0 1` — A typical structure where White examines the thematic sacrifice **Bxh7+** followed by Ng5+ and Qh5. → [The Greek Gift](../07-Attacking-Play/the-greek-gift-bxh7.md)

---

*All positions are legal and the stated tactics are sound. Open any FEN on Lichess by replacing spaces with underscores after `https://lichess.org/analysis/`.*

# 📖 Glossary of Chess Terms

Every important term used in this curriculum, defined in one line, linked to its full file where one exists. Terms are grouped: **Tactics · Strategy · Endgames · Calculation · General**.

---

## Tactical motifs

| Term | One-line definition | Full file |
|---|---|---|
| **Fork** | One piece attacks two or more targets at once. | [fork](03-Tactics/fork.md) |
| **Royal fork** | A fork hitting the enemy king **and** queen (usually by a knight). | [fork](03-Tactics/fork.md) |
| **Family fork** | A knight fork hitting king, queen and rook together. | [fork](03-Tactics/fork.md) |
| **Pin** | A piece cannot/should not move because a more valuable piece sits behind it. | [pin](03-Tactics/pin.md) |
| **Absolute pin** | A pin against the king — the pinned piece is **illegal** to move. | [pin](03-Tactics/pin.md) |
| **Relative pin** | A pin against a valuable (non-king) piece — legal but costly to break. | [pin](03-Tactics/pin.md) |
| **Skewer** | The reverse pin: the more valuable piece is in front and must move, exposing a piece behind. | [skewer](03-Tactics/skewer.md) |
| **Discovered attack** | Moving one piece unveils an attack from a piece behind it. | [discovered-attack](03-Tactics/discovered-attack.md) |
| **Discovered check** | A discovered attack where the unveiled piece gives check. | [discovered-check](03-Tactics/discovered-check.md) |
| **Double check** | Both the moving piece and the unveiled piece give check; the king **must** move. | [discovered-check](03-Tactics/discovered-check.md) |
| **Double attack** | Two simultaneous threats the opponent cannot both meet (a fork is one kind). | [double-attack](03-Tactics/double-attack.md) |
| **Decoy (attraction)** | Luring an enemy piece, often the king, **to** a bad square via sacrifice. | [decoy](03-Tactics/decoy.md) |
| **Deflection** | Forcing a defender **away** from a duty it was performing. | [deflection](03-Tactics/deflection.md) |
| **Removing the defender** | Capturing or chasing the piece that guards a key square or piece. | [removing-the-defender](03-Tactics/removing-the-defender.md) |
| **Overloading** | A defender given too many duties; attack one and the others collapse. | [overloading](03-Tactics/overloading.md) |
| **Interference (obstruction)** | Interposing a piece to cut the line between a defender and what it defends. | [interference](03-Tactics/interference.md) |
| **Clearance sacrifice** | Vacating a square, file, rank or diagonal so another piece can use it. | [clearance-sacrifice](03-Tactics/clearance-sacrifice.md) |
| **Zwischenzug (intermezzo)** | An in-between move (usually a check or threat) inserted before the expected reply. | [zwischenzug](03-Tactics/zwischenzug.md) |
| **X-ray** | A long-range piece acts **through** an intervening piece on a line. | [x-ray](03-Tactics/x-ray.md) |
| **Windmill (see-saw)** | A repeating discovered-check mechanism that harvests material. | [windmill](03-Tactics/windmill.md) |
| **Trapped piece** | An enemy piece restricted until it can be captured. | [trapped-piece](03-Tactics/trapped-piece.md) |
| **Back-rank mate** | Mate on the 1st/8th rank when the king is boxed in by its own pawns. | [back-rank-mate](03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md) |
| **Luft** | An escape square made for the king (e.g. h3/h6) to avoid back-rank mate. | [back-rank-mate](03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md) |
| **Smothered mate** | A knight mates a king hemmed in by its own pieces (Philidor's legacy). | [smothered-mate](03-Tactics/smothered-mate.md) |
| **Desperado** | A doomed piece grabs material or creates a threat before it is lost. | [desperado](03-Tactics/desperado.md) |
| **Underpromotion** | Promoting to knight, rook or bishop instead of a queen for a tactical reason. | [underpromotion](03-Tactics/underpromotion.md) |
| **Combination** | A forcing sequence of moves (often with a sacrifice) yielding a concrete gain. | [combinations](03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md) |
| **Sham sacrifice** | A "sacrifice" whose material is regained by force (vs a real/positional sac). | [combinations](03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md) |
| **CCT** | Checks, Captures, Threats — the forcing moves to scan first every move. | [combinations](03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md) |
| **LPDO** | "Loose Pieces Drop Off" — undefended pieces invite double attacks. | [double-attack](03-Tactics/double-attack.md) |

---

## Positional / strategic terms

| Term | One-line definition | Full file |
|---|---|---|
| **Pawn structure** | The pawn skeleton that dictates plans for both sides. | [pawn-structures](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/pawn-structures.md) |
| **Isolated pawn (IQP)** | A pawn with no friendly pawns on adjacent files; weak square in front, but dynamic. | [pawn weaknesses](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/isolated-doubled-backward-pawns.md) |
| **Doubled pawns** | Two friendly pawns on the same file. | [pawn weaknesses](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/isolated-doubled-backward-pawns.md) |
| **Backward pawn** | A pawn that cannot safely advance and cannot be defended by a pawn. | [pawn weaknesses](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/isolated-doubled-backward-pawns.md) |
| **Passed pawn** | A pawn with no enemy pawns able to stop it on its file or adjacent files. | [passed-pawns](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/passed-pawns.md) |
| **Outpost** | A square (often on the 5th/6th rank) that can't be attacked by an enemy pawn — ideal for a knight. | [weak squares & outposts](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/weak-squares-and-outposts.md) |
| **Open / half-open file** | A file with no pawns / no friendly pawns — a highway for rooks. | [open files](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/open-and-half-open-files.md) |
| **Bishop pair** | Owning both bishops — a long-term edge, especially in open positions. | [the bishop pair](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/the-bishop-pair.md) |
| **Good / bad bishop** | A bishop free of / blocked by its own pawns. | [good & bad bishops](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/good-and-bad-bishops.md) |
| **Minority attack** | Advancing fewer pawns against more to create a weakness. | [minority attack](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/the-minority-attack.md) |
| **Prophylaxis** | Preventing the opponent's plan before pursuing your own. | [prophylaxis](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/prophylaxis-and-restriction.md) |
| **Two weaknesses** | Win by creating a second front so the defense is overstretched. | [two weaknesses](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/the-two-weaknesses-principle.md) |
| **Initiative** | The ability to make threats and force the opponent to react. | [sacrificing for initiative](07-Attacking-Play/piece-sacrifices-for-initiative.md) |
| **Tempo** | A unit of time (one move); "gaining a tempo" = developing while making a threat. | [maneuvering](04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/maneuvering-and-improving-pieces.md) |

---

## Endgame terms

| Term | One-line definition | Full file |
|---|---|---|
| **Opposition** | Kings face off with one square between; the side **not** to move controls key squares. | [the opposition](05-Endgames/the-opposition.md) |
| **Distant / diagonal opposition** | Opposition held across several squares or on a diagonal. | [the opposition](05-Endgames/the-opposition.md) |
| **Triangulation** | A king maneuver to **lose a tempo** and hand the move to the opponent (forcing zugzwang). | [triangulation](05-Endgames/triangulation.md) |
| **Zugzwang** | A position where any move worsens your position — the duty to move is a liability. | [zugzwang](05-Endgames/zugzwang.md) |
| **Mutual (reciprocal) zugzwang** | A position bad for **whichever** side is to move. | [zugzwang](05-Endgames/zugzwang.md) |
| **Key squares** | Squares the stronger king must reach to force a pawn through. | [key squares](05-Endgames/key-squares-and-rule-of-the-square.md) |
| **Rule of the square** | A quick test of whether a lone king can catch a passed pawn. | [key squares](05-Endgames/key-squares-and-rule-of-the-square.md) |
| **Lucena position** | The fundamental winning R+P vs R method — "building a bridge." | [rook endgames](05-Endgames/rook-endgames.md) |
| **Philidor position** | The fundamental drawing R vs R+P defense — 3rd-rank then checks from behind. | [rook endgames](05-Endgames/rook-endgames.md) |
| **Fortress** | A drawn setup the stronger side cannot break despite a material deficit. | [the fortress](05-Endgames/the-fortress.md) |
| **Breakthrough** | A pawn sacrifice that creates an unstoppable passed pawn. | [pawn breakthrough](05-Endgames/pawn-breakthrough.md) |
| **Wrong-colored bishop** | A rook-pawn + bishop that can't control the queening square = draw. | [minor-piece endgames](05-Endgames/minor-piece-endgames.md) |

---

## Calculation / general terms

| Term | One-line definition | Full file |
|---|---|---|
| **Candidate moves** | The shortlist of plausible moves you calculate in a position. | [candidate moves](02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/candidate-moves-and-the-tree.md) |
| **Tree of analysis** | The branching structure of variations you calculate. | [calculation method](02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/calculation-method.md) |
| **Visualization** | Seeing the board and future positions in your mind without moving pieces. | [visualization](02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/visualization-training.md) |
| **Evaluation** | Judging who stands better and by how much (material, king safety, activity, structure). | [evaluating positions](02-Board-Vision-and-Calculation/evaluating-positions.md) |
| **Tabiya** | A standard, frequently-reached opening position from which plans branch. | [repertoire](06-Openings/building-a-repertoire.md) |
| **Novelty (TN)** | A new move in a known theoretical position. | [2500-2800 band](09-Training-Plan/06-band-2500-2800.md) |
| **Tilt** | Emotionally-driven poor play after a setback. | [psychology](10-Tournament-and-Psychology/psychology-tilt-and-mental-game.md) |
| **Deliberate practice** | Focused training at the edge of your ability with immediate feedback. | [deliberate practice](01-Mindset-and-Study-Method/deliberate-practice.md) |
| **Title norms** | Performance results required to earn FM/IM/GM titles. | [the rating ladder](00-Start-Here/the-rating-ladder-explained.md) |

---

> Notation reminder: **K**=king, **Q**=queen, **R**=rook, **B**=bishop, **N**=knight, pawns have no letter. `+` = check, `#` = checkmate, `x` = capture, `O-O` = kingside castling, `O-O-O` = queenside, `=Q` = promotion. In this guide's ASCII boards, **UPPERCASE = White**, *lowercase* = Black, `.` = empty square.

# 📖 Glossary of Chess Terms

Every important term used in this curriculum, defined in one line, linked to its full file where one exists. Terms are grouped: **Tactics · Strategy · Endgames · Calculation · General**.

---

## Tactical motifs

| Term | One-line definition | Full file |
|---|---|---|
| **Fork** | One piece attacks two or more targets at once. | [fork](03-Tactics/fork.md) |
| **Royal fork** | A fork hitting the enemy king **and** queen (usually by a knight). | [fork](03-Tactics/fork.md) |
| **Family fork** | A knight fork hitting king, queen and rook together. | [fork](03-Tactics/fork.md) |
| **Pin** | A piece cannot/should not move because a more valuable piece sits behind it. | [pin](03-Tactics/pin.md) |
| **Absolute pin** | A pin against the king — the pinned piece is **illegal** to move. | [pin](03-Tactics/pin.md) |
| **Relative pin** | A pin against a valuable (non-king) piece — legal but costly to break. | [pin](03-Tactics/pin.md) |
| **Skewer** | The reverse pin: the more valuable piece is in front and must move, exposing a piece behind. | [skewer](03-Tactics/skewer.md) |
| **Discovered attack** | Moving one piece unveils an attack from a piece behind it. | [discovered-attack](03-Tactics/discovered-attack.md) |
| **Discovered check** | A discovered attack where the unveiled piece gives check. | [discovered-check](03-Tactics/discovered-check.md) |
| **Double check** | Both the moving piece and the unveiled piece give check; the king **must** move. | [discovered-check](03-Tactics/discovered-check.md) |
| **Double attack** | Two simultaneous threats the opponent cannot both meet (a fork is one kind). | [double-attack](03-Tactics/double-attack.md) |
| **Decoy (attraction)** | Luring an enemy piece, often the king, **to** a bad square via sacrifice. | [decoy](03-Tactics/decoy.md) |
| **Deflection** | Forcing a defender **away** from a duty it was performing. | [deflection](03-Tactics/deflection.md) |
| **Removing the defender** | Capturing or chasing the piece that guards a key square or piece. | [removing-the-defender](03-Tactics/removing-the-defender.md) |
| **Overloading** | A defender given too many duties; attack one and the others collapse. | [overloading](03-Tactics/overloading.md) |
| **Interference (obstruction)** | Interposing a piece to cut the line between a defender and what it defends. | [interference](03-Tactics/interference.md) |
| **Clearance sacrifice** | Vacating a square, file, rank or diagonal so another piece can use it. | [clearance-sacrifice](03-Tactics/clearance-sacrifice.md) |
| **Zwischenzug (intermezzo)** | An in-between move (usually a check or threat) inserted before the expected reply. | [zwischenzug](03-Tactics/zwischenzug.md) |
| **X-ray** | A long-range piece acts **through** an intervening piece on a line. | [x-ray](03-Tactics/x-ray.md) |
| **Windmill (see-saw)** | A repeating discovered-check mechanism that harvests material. | [windmill](03-Tactics/windmill.md) |
| **Trapped piece** | An enemy piece restricted until it can be captured. | [trapped-piece](03-Tactics/trapped-piece.md) |
| **Back-rank mate** | Mate on the 1st/8th rank when the king is boxed in by its own pawns. | [back-rank-mate](03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md) |
| **Luft** | An escape square made for the king (e.g. h3/h6) to avoid back-rank mate. | [back-rank-mate](03-Tactics/back-rank-mate.md) |
| **Smothered mate** | A knight mates a king hemmed in by its own pieces (Philidor's legacy). | [smothered-mate](03-Tactics/smothered-mate.md) |
| **Desperado** | A doomed piece grabs material or creates a threat before it is lost. | [desperado](03-Tactics/desperado.md) |
| **Underpromotion** | Promoting to knight, rook or bishop instead of a queen for a tactical reason. | [underpromotion](03-Tactics/underpromotion.md) |
| **Combination** | A forcing sequence of moves (often with a sacrifice) yielding a concrete gain. | [combinations](03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md) |
| **Sham sacrifice** | A "sacrifice" whose material is regained by force (vs a real/positional sac). | [combinations](03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md) |
| **CCT** | Checks, Captures, Threats — the forcing moves to scan first every move. | [combinations](03-Tactics/combinations-and-sacrifices.md) |
| **LPDO** | "Loose Pieces Drop Off" — undefended pieces invite double attacks. | [double-attack](03-Tactics/double-attack.md) |

---

content.content:
1	# The Fork
2	
3	> A fork is a single move in which one piece or pawn attacks two or more enemy targets at the same time, so the opponent cannot save them all.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
6	**Prerequisites:** [Tactics fundamentals](tactics-fundamentals.md), [Loose pieces](loose-pieces.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	A fork exploits the simplest truth in chess: a move can only do one thing at a time. If you attack two enemy pieces with one move, your opponent gets a single reply. They rescue one target — you take the other.
11	
12	Every piece can fork. The knight is the most famous forker because it jumps, so it can hit two pieces that are far apart and even pieces that defend each other. But pawns, bishops, rooks, queens, and even the king fork constantly. The strongest forks hit the **king** (a check that *must* be answered) plus a valuable, undefended piece. That is exactly why the **royal fork** (king + queen) and the **family fork** (king + queen + rook) win so much material: the king move is forced, and the second target falls for free.
13	
14	## Why it matters
15	
16	Forks are the highest-frequency winning tactic below master level. Most decisive games at 1500–2000 turn on a fork that one side missed — failing to spot their own, or walking into the opponent's. Training your eye to read knight-distance relationships and loose-piece geometry instantly will earn you more rating points than almost any opening study. A single royal fork that nets a queen for a knight ends the game on the spot.
17	
18	## Diagrams
19	
20	```
21	8 . . . . . k . r
22	7 . . . . . . . .
23	6 . . . . . . N .
24	5 . . . . . . . .
25	4 . . . . . . . .
26	3 . . . . . . . .
27	2 . . . . . . . .
28	1 . . . . . K . .
29	  a b c d e f g h
30	```
31	*Knight on g6 checks the king on f8 and simultaneously attacks the rook on h8 — a textbook king + rook fork.*
32	**FEN:** 5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1
33	Side to move: White.
34	[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)
35	
36	The knight on g6 attacks **f8 (check)** and **h8** — both a knight's-move away. Black must answer the check first, for example **1...Ke7**, and then **2.Nxh8** wins the rook clean. The knight survives precisely because the king has to step out of check before it can defend h8.
37	
38	```
39	8 . . . . k . . .
40	7 . . . . . . . .
41	6 . . . . q . . .
42	5 . N . . . . . .
43	4 . . . . . . . .
44	3 . . . . . . . .
45	2 . . . . . . . .
46	1 . . . . . . . K
47	  a b c d e f g h
48	```
49	*Knight on b5 is one hop from the royal fork: 1.Nc7+ forks the king on e8 and the queen on e6.*
50	**FEN:** 4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1
51	Side to move: White.
52	[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)
53	
54	White plays **1.Nc7+!** From c7 the knight attacks **e8 (check)** and **e6 (queen)** — its squares from c7 are a6, a8, b5, d5, e6, e8. The king must move, e.g. **1...Kd8**, and then **2.Nxe6+** wins the queen. This is the royal fork in its purest form: a one-move knight check that also hits the queen.
55	
56	## Patterns and sub-types
57	
58	- **Royal fork:** a knight checks the king and attacks the queen at once. Wins the queen outright.
59	- **Family fork:** one knight check hits king + queen + rook, typically landing on e7, f7, c7, or d6 deep in the enemy camp.
60	- **Pawn fork:** a pawn advances to attack the two squares diagonally in front of it; cheap and brutal because the attacker is worth one point.
61	- **Bishop fork:** the bishop hits two pieces on the same colored diagonal, often king + rook in opposite corners.
62	- **Rook fork:** a rook on a rank or file attacks two undefended pieces at once.
63	- **Queen fork:** the queen's range makes it the most flexible forker — usually a check plus a hit on a loose piece.
64	- **Absolute fork:** one target is the king (a check) — the most forcing, since the reply is not optional.
65	- **Relative fork:** neither target is the king, so the defender may have a counter-threat.
66	
67	## How to spot it
68	
69	```mermaid
70	flowchart TD
71	  A[My move to play] --> B[List enemy loose or high value pieces]
72	  B --> C{Two targets a knight move apart}
73	  C -->|yes| D[Check a knight fork]
74	  C -->|no| E{Two targets on one line or diagonal}
75	  E -->|yes| F[Check rook bishop or queen fork]
76	  E -->|no| G{A pawn push hits two pieces}
77	  G -->|yes| H[Play the pawn fork]
78	  D --> I{Is the landing square safe}
79	  F --> I
80	  I -->|yes| J[Execute and win material]
81	  I -->|no| K[Look for another tactic]
82	```
83	
84	Flag the enemy **king and queen first**, then any **undefended** piece, then ask whether one of your pieces can reach a square that touches two of them.
85	
86	## Worked examples
87	
88	### Example 1 — Pawn fork
89	
90	```
91	8 . . . . . . . k
92	7 . . . . . . . .
93	6 . . . . . . . .
94	5 . . n . b . . .
95	4 . . . . . . . .
96	3 . . . P . . . .
97	2 . . . . . . . .
98	1 . . . . . . . K
99	  a b c d e f g h
100	```
101	*White's pawn on d3 pushes to d4, attacking the knight on c5 and the bishop on e5 at the same time.*
102	**FEN:** 7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1
103	Side to move: White.
104	[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)
105	
106	**1.d4!** A pawn on d4 attacks **c5** and **e5** — both black minors at once. Black saves one, say **1...Bd6**, and White plays **2.dxc5** winning a clean piece. A one-point pawn has netted a three-point minor.
107	
108	### Example 2 — Family fork
109	
110	```
111	8 . . r . . . k .
112	7 . . . . . . p p
113	6 . . N . . . . .
114	5 . . . . . q . .
115	4 . . . . . . . .
116	3 . . . . . . . .
117	2 . . . . . . . .
118	1 . . . . . . K .
119	  a b c d e f g h
120	```
121	*Knight on c6 jumps to e7, hitting the king on g8, the queen on f5, and the rook on c8 — the full family fork.*
122	**FEN:** 2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1 w - - 0 1
123	Side to move: White.
124	[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1_w_-_-_0_1)
125	
126	**1.Ne7+!** From e7 the knight attacks **g8 (check)**, **f5 (queen)**, and **c8 (rook)** — its squares from e7 are c6, c8, d5, f5, g6, g8. The king must escape the check, e.g. **1...Kf8** or **1...Kh8**, and then **2.Nxf5** wins the queen (the rook was also forked, but the queen is the bigger prize). One knight check, three targets, game over.
127	
128	## Common mistakes
129	
130	- Forking onto a square where the enemy can simply capture the forking piece — it hangs.
131	- Using a rook, bishop, or queen to "fork" two pieces that **defend each other**: the opponent recaptures and you have only traded.
132	- Forgetting that a relative (non-check) fork lets the opponent reply with their own check or a bigger threat.
133	- Missing forks **against you** — always scan enemy knight landing squares before you move.
134	- Grabbing the lower-value target when a check would have let you win the higher one with tempo.
135	
136	## Train it
137	
138	- **Lichess puzzle themes:** the [Fork](https://lichess.org/training/fork) theme, plus [Hanging piece](https://lichess.org/training/hangingPiece). Do **20 fork puzzles per day** for two weeks.
139	- **Knight-vision drill:** place a lone knight on an empty board and, for 5 minutes daily, name every square it attacks from each square until it is instant.
140	- **Books:** *Chess Tactics for Students* (John Bain), the fork chapters; *1001 Chess Exercises for Beginners* (Frank Erwich); *Understanding Chess Tactics* (Weteschnik) for the deeper loose-pieces principle.
141	- **Game review:** after each game, find one fork you or your opponent missed. Log one per game.
142	
143	## Exercises
144	
145	1. **FEN:** `5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the rook.
146	2. **FEN:** `7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a piece with a pawn.
147	3. **FEN:** `4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Royal fork: win the queen with check.
148	4. **FEN:** `2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Find the family fork.
149	5. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1` — White to move. A queen fork: win the knight with check.
150	
151	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
152	
153	1. The knight on g6 already attacks **f8 (check)** and **h8**. After the forced **1...Ke7** (or Ke8/Kg8), **2.Nxh8** wins the rook clean.
154	2. **1.d4!** A pawn on d4 attacks **c5** and **e5**, forking the knight and bishop. Black saves one; **2.dxc5** (or dxe5) wins a clean minor piece.
155	3. **1.Nc7+!** checks the king on e8 and attacks the queen on e6. After **1...Kd8** (or Kf8/Kd7), **2.Nxe6+** wins the queen.
156	4. **1.Ne7+!** forks the king on g8, the queen on f5, and the rook on c8. After **1...Kf8** (or Kh8), **2.Nxf5** wins the queen.
157	5. **1.Qh5+!** The queen on h5 checks the king on e8 along the h5–e8 diagonal (h5–g6–f7–e8) and attacks the knight on b5 along the 5th rank. After the king moves, **2.Qxb5** wins the knight.
158	
159	</details>
160	
161	## Related
162	
163	- [Double attack](double-attack.md) — the family the fork belongs to.
164	- [Discovered attack](discovered-attack.md) — two attacks from two different pieces.
165	- [Skewer](skewer.md) and [Pin](pin.md) — line tactics often confused with forks.
166	- [Deflection](deflection.md) and [Loose pieces](loose-pieces.md) — what creates forkable targets.
167

file.content:
# The Fork

> A fork is a single move in which one piece or pawn attacks two or more enemy targets at the same time, so the opponent cannot save them all.

**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
**Prerequisites:** [Tactics fundamentals](tactics-fundamentals.md), [Loose pieces](loose-pieces.md)

## The idea

A fork exploits the simplest truth in chess: a move can only do one thing at a time. If you attack two enemy pieces with one move, your opponent gets a single reply. They rescue one target — you take the other.

Every piece can fork. The knight is the most famous forker because it jumps, so it can hit two pieces that are far apart and even pieces that defend each other. But pawns, bishops, rooks, queens, and even the king fork constantly. The strongest forks hit the **king** (a check that *must* be answered) plus a valuable, undefended piece. That is exactly why the **royal fork** (king + queen) and the **family fork** (king + queen + rook) win so much material: the king move is forced, and the second target falls for free.

## Why it matters

Forks are the highest-frequency winning tactic below master level. Most decisive games at 1500–2000 turn on a fork that one side missed — failing to spot their own, or walking into the opponent's. Training your eye to read knight-distance relationships and loose-piece geometry instantly will earn you more rating points than almost any opening study. A single royal fork that nets a queen for a knight ends the game on the spot.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . k . r
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . N .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . K . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Knight on g6 checks the king on f8 and simultaneously attacks the rook on h8 — a textbook king + rook fork.*
**FEN:** 5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2_w_-_-_0_1)

The knight on g6 attacks **f8 (check)** and **h8** — both a knight's-move away. Black must answer the check first, for example **1...Ke7**, and then **2.Nxh8** wins the rook clean. The knight survives precisely because the king has to step out of check before it can defend h8.

```
8 . . . . k . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . q . . .
5 . N . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Knight on b5 is one hop from the royal fork: 1.Nc7+ forks the king on e8 and the queen on e6.*
**FEN:** 4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

White plays **1.Nc7+!** From c7 the knight attacks **e8 (check)** and **e6 (queen)** — its squares from c7 are a6, a8, b5, d5, e6, e8. The king must move, e.g. **1...Kd8**, and then **2.Nxe6+** wins the queen. This is the royal fork in its purest form: a one-move knight check that also hits the queen.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Royal fork:** a knight checks the king and attacks the queen at once. Wins the queen outright.
- **Family fork:** one knight check hits king + queen + rook, typically landing on e7, f7, c7, or d6 deep in the enemy camp.
- **Pawn fork:** a pawn advances to attack the two squares diagonally in front of it; cheap and brutal because the attacker is worth one point.
- **Bishop fork:** the bishop hits two pieces on the same colored diagonal, often king + rook in opposite corners.
- **Rook fork:** a rook on a rank or file attacks two undefended pieces at once.
- **Queen fork:** the queen's range makes it the most flexible forker — usually a check plus a hit on a loose piece.
- **Absolute fork:** one target is the king (a check) — the most forcing, since the reply is not optional.
- **Relative fork:** neither target is the king, so the defender may have a counter-threat.

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[My move to play] --> B[List enemy loose or high value pieces]
  B --> C{Two targets a knight move apart}
  C -->|yes| D[Check a knight fork]
  C -->|no| E{Two targets on one line or diagonal}
  E -->|yes| F[Check rook bishop or queen fork]
  E -->|no| G{A pawn push hits two pieces}
  G -->|yes| H[Play the pawn fork]
  D --> I{Is the landing square safe}
  F --> I
  I -->|yes| J[Execute and win material]
  I -->|no| K[Look for another tactic]
```

Flag the enemy **king and queen first**, then any **undefended** piece, then ask whether one of your pieces can reach a square that touches two of them.

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — Pawn fork

```
8 . . . . . . . k
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . n . b . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . P . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . . K
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White's pawn on d3 pushes to d4, attacking the knight on c5 and the bishop on e5 at the same time.*
**FEN:** 7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.d4!** A pawn on d4 attacks **c5** and **e5** — both black minors at once. Black saves one, say **1...Bd6**, and White plays **2.dxc5** winning a clean piece. A one-point pawn has netted a three-point minor.

### Example 2 — Family fork

```
8 . . r . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . N . . . . .
5 . . . . . q . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . . . .
1 . . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Knight on c6 jumps to e7, hitting the king on g8, the queen on f5, and the rook on c8 — the full family fork.*
**FEN:** 2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Ne7+!** From e7 the knight attacks **g8 (check)**, **f5 (queen)**, and **c8 (rook)** — its squares from e7 are c6, c8, d5, f5, g6, g8. The king must escape the check, e.g. **1...Kf8** or **1...Kh8**, and then **2.Nxf5** wins the queen (the rook was also forked, but the queen is the bigger prize). One knight check, three targets, game over.

## Common mistakes

- Forking onto a square where the enemy can simply capture the forking piece — it hangs.
- Using a rook, bishop, or queen to "fork" two pieces that **defend each other**: the opponent recaptures and you have only traded.
- Forgetting that a relative (non-check) fork lets the opponent reply with their own check or a bigger threat.
- Missing forks **against you** — always scan enemy knight landing squares before you move.
- Grabbing the lower-value target when a check would have let you win the higher one with tempo.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** the [Fork](https://lichess.org/training/fork) theme, plus [Hanging piece](https://lichess.org/training/hangingPiece). Do **20 fork puzzles per day** for two weeks.
- **Knight-vision drill:** place a lone knight on an empty board and, for 5 minutes daily, name every square it attacks from each square until it is instant.
- **Books:** *Chess Tactics for Students* (John Bain), the fork chapters; *1001 Chess Exercises for Beginners* (Frank Erwich); *Understanding Chess Tactics* (Weteschnik) for the deeper loose-pieces principle.
- **Game review:** after each game, find one fork you or your opponent missed. Log one per game.

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `5k1r/8/6N1/8/8/8/8/5K2 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the rook.
2. **FEN:** `7k/8/8/2n1b3/8/3P4/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a piece with a pawn.
3. **FEN:** `4k3/8/4q3/1N6/8/8/8/7K w - - 0 1` — White to move. Royal fork: win the queen with check.
4. **FEN:** `2r3k1/6pp/2N5/5q2/8/8/8/6K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Find the family fork.
5. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1` — White to move. A queen fork: win the knight with check.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. The knight on g6 already attacks **f8 (check)** and **h8**. After the forced **1...Ke7** (or Ke8/Kg8), **2.Nxh8** wins the rook clean.
2. **1.d4!** A pawn on d4 attacks **c5** and **e5**, forking the knight and bishop. Black saves one; **2.dxc5** (or dxe5) wins a clean minor piece.
3. **1.Nc7+!** checks the king on e8 and attacks the queen on e6. After **1...Kd8** (or Kf8/Kd7), **2.Nxe6+** wins the queen.
4. **1.Ne7+!** forks the king on g8, the queen on f5, and the rook on c8. After **1...Kf8** (or Kh8), **2.Nxf5** wins the queen.
5. **1.Qh5+!** The queen on h5 checks the king on e8 along the h5–e8 diagonal (h5–g6–f7–e8) and attacks the knight on b5 along the 5th rank. After the king moves, **2.Qxb5** wins the knight.

</details>

## Related

- [Double attack](double-attack.md) — the family the fork belongs to.
- [Discovered attack](discovered-attack.md) — two attacks from two different pieces.
- [Skewer](skewer.md) and [Pin](pin.md) — line tactics often confused with forks.
- [Deflection](deflection.md) and [Loose pieces](loose-pieces.md) — what creates forkable targets.

Concept tags: [[fork]] [[double-attack]] [[knight-fork]] [[royal-fork]] [[family-fork]] [[pawn-fork]]

input.content:
# Loose Pieces (LPDO — "Loose Pieces Drop Off")

> A loose piece is any piece that is undefended (or under-defended). Loose pieces are the raw material of almost every tactic — they are what forks, double attacks, pins and skewers feed on.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
**Prerequisites:** none

## The idea

Most tactics do not appear out of nowhere — they appear because a piece is **undefended**. GM John Nunn coined the rule **LPDO: Loose Pieces Drop Off.** If two of your pieces are loose, the opponent only needs one move that attacks both, and one of them falls.

So strong players develop a constant background habit: every move, they scan the board and ask, *"Which pieces — mine and my opponent's — are undefended?"* Those squares are where double attacks, forks and skewers will land. Reduce your own loose pieces and you stop walking into tactics; spot the opponent's and you start finding them.

## Why it matters

Below master level, the single biggest source of swings is a loose piece getting hit by a fork or a queen double-attack. You do not even need a forcing combination — often a centralizing queen move attacks two undefended pieces at once and wins one for free. Training "loose-piece vision" is the cheapest way to both **stop blundering** and **start winning material**.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . r . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . r
2 . . . . . . P P
1 Q . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Both black rooks (c8 and h3) are loose. White's queen finds one square that attacks both.*
**FEN:** 2r3k1/6pp/8/8/8/7r/6PP/Q5K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/2r3k1/6pp/8/8/8/7r/6PP/Q5K1_w_-_-_0_1)

White plays **1.Qc3!** From c3 the queen attacks **c8** up the open c-file and **h3** along the third rank. The two rooks are undefended, so this is a classic double attack: Black can save only one. After, say, **1...Rhc8 2.Qxc8+** (or **1...Rxc3** is impossible — the rook on h3 cannot reach c3) White wins a whole rook. Two loose pieces, one fork, game over.

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . b . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . n . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . Q . . P P
1 . . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Black's bishop (b6) and knight (e4) are both undefended. The queen forks them.*
**FEN:** 6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Qb4!** attacks the bishop on **b6** (up the b-file) and the knight on **e4** (along the fourth rank). Neither minor piece is defended, so after Black rescues one — **1...Bd8 2.Qxe4** — White wins a clean piece. The combination exists *only* because both minors were loose; defend either one and there is no tactic.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Two loose pieces on a line:** a queen, rook or bishop attacks both at once (a [double attack](double-attack.md) / [fork](fork.md)).
- **Loose piece + check:** the most lethal pattern — a check the opponent *must* answer, and the second move grabs the loose piece with tempo.
- **Loose piece + mate threat:** the threat forces a defensive move; meanwhile the loose piece hangs.
- **Under-defended (not fully loose):** a piece defended once but attacked twice — pile up another attacker.
- **Tactically loose:** a piece "defended" by a pinned, overloaded, or about-to-be-deflected guard is effectively loose. See [overloading](overloading.md) and [deflection](deflection.md).

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[After every opponent move] --> B[List all undefended pieces both colours]
  B --> C{Any of MY pieces loose}
  C -->|yes| D[Defend it or move it before it is hit]
  C -->|no| E{Two ENEMY pieces loose}
  E -->|yes| F[Find a square or check that hits both]
  E -->|no| G{One enemy piece loose}
  G -->|yes| H[Look for a check that also wins it]
  G -->|no| I[No LPDO target this move]
```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — defend your own loose piece first

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . b . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . B . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Both bishops sit loose on the a1–h8 / a3–f8 diagonals. Whoever moves can hunt — so check your own first.*
**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/2b5/8/8/5PPP/2B3K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/8/2b5/8/8/5PPP/2B3K1_w_-_-_0_1)

Here neither side has an immediate double attack, but the lesson is the **scan**: both bishops are loose, so a single tempo (a future queen or rook arriving with a threat on c5 or c1) could win one. The habit of noticing this *before* it becomes tactical is what prevents blunders.

### Example 2 — loose piece + check

```
8 . . . . k . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . n . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . Q . . .
1 . . . . K . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The knight on b5 is loose, and the black king is exposed — combine them.*
**FEN:** 4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Qh5+!** checks the king along the h5–e8 diagonal **and** attacks the loose knight on b5 along the fifth rank. Black must answer the check; then **2.Qxb5** scoops the knight. The loose piece plus a forcing check is the bread-and-butter winning pattern.

## Common mistakes

- Leaving a piece undefended "just for a moment" — that moment is exactly when the fork lands.
- Counting a piece as defended when its defender is **pinned**, **overloaded**, or about to be **deflected**.
- Only scanning the opponent's loose pieces and not your own.
- Trading into a position where your remaining pieces are scattered and loose.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle theme:** [Hanging piece](https://lichess.org/training/hangingPiece). Do **15–20 per day**.
- **Daily 30-second scan drill:** load any middlegame position and, before reading the solution, name every undefended piece on the board.
- **In your own games:** the blunder-check before every move is simply *"Is anything of mine loose, and can it be hit?"* See [the thinking process](../flowcharts/the-thinking-process.md).

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `2r3k1/6pp/8/8/8/7r/6PP/Q5K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a rook.
2. **FEN:** `6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a minor piece.
3. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the knight with a check.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Qc3!** double-attacks the rook on c8 (c-file) and the rook on h3 (third rank). Black saves one; White takes the other, winning a full rook.
2. **1.Qb4!** attacks the bishop on b6 and the knight on e4. After Black moves one, e.g. **1...Bd8 2.Qxe4**, White wins a clean piece.
3. **1.Qh5+!** checks the king on e8 and attacks the loose knight on b5. After the king moves, **2.Qxb5** wins the knight.

</details>

## Related

- [Fork](fork.md) and [Double attack](double-attack.md) — the tactics loose pieces enable.
- [Overloading](overloading.md) and [Deflection](deflection.md) — how a "defended" piece becomes loose.
- [Pin](pin.md) — a pinned defender means the piece it guards is effectively loose.

Concept tags: [[loose-pieces]] [[lpdo]] [[double-attack]] [[fork]] [[hanging-piece]]

# Loose Pieces (LPDO — "Loose Pieces Drop Off")

> A loose piece is any piece that is undefended (or under-defended). Loose pieces are the raw material of almost every tactic — they are what forks, double attacks, pins and skewers feed on.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
**Prerequisites:** none

## The idea

Most tactics do not appear out of nowhere — they appear because a piece is **undefended**. GM John Nunn coined the rule **LPDO: Loose Pieces Drop Off.** If two of your pieces are loose, the opponent only needs one move that attacks both, and one of them falls.

So strong players develop a constant background habit: every move, they scan the board and ask, *"Which pieces — mine and my opponent's — are undefended?"* Those squares are where double attacks, forks and skewers will land. Reduce your own loose pieces and you stop walking into tactics; spot the opponent's and you start finding them.

## Why it matters

Below master level, the single biggest source of swings is a loose piece getting hit by a fork or a queen double-attack. You do not even need a forcing combination — often a centralizing queen move attacks two undefended pieces at once and wins one for free. Training "loose-piece vision" is the cheapest way to both **stop blundering** and **start winning material**.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . r . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . r
2 . . . . . . P P
1 Q . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Both black rooks (c8 and h3) are loose. White's queen finds one square that attacks both.*
**FEN:** 2r3k1/6pp/8/8/8/7r/6PP/Q5K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/2r3k1/6pp/8/8/8/7r/6PP/Q5K1_w_-_-_0_1)

White plays **1.Qc3!** From c3 the queen attacks **c8** up the open c-file and **h3** along the third rank. The two rooks are undefended, so this is a classic double attack: Black can save only one. After, say, **1...Rhc8 2.Qxc8+** (or **1...Rxc3** is impossible — the rook on h3 cannot reach c3) White wins a whole rook. Two loose pieces, one fork, game over.

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . b . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . n . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . Q . . P P
1 . . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Black's bishop (b6) and knight (e4) are both undefended. The queen forks them.*
**FEN:** 6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Qb4!** attacks the bishop on **b6** (up the b-file) and the knight on **e4** (along the fourth rank). Neither minor piece is defended, so after Black rescues one — **1...Bd8 2.Qxe4** — White wins a clean piece. The combination exists *only* because both minors were loose; defend either one and there is no tactic.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Two loose pieces on a line:** a queen, rook or bishop attacks both at once (a [double attack](double-attack.md) / [fork](fork.md)).
- **Loose piece + check:** the most lethal pattern — a check the opponent *must* answer, and the second move grabs the loose piece with tempo.
- **Loose piece + mate threat:** the threat forces a defensive move; meanwhile the loose piece hangs.
- **Under-defended (not fully loose):** a piece defended once but attacked twice — pile up another attacker.
- **Tactically loose:** a piece "defended" by a pinned, overloaded, or about-to-be-deflected guard is effectively loose. See [overloading](overloading.md) and [deflection](deflection.md).

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[After every opponent move] --> B[List all undefended pieces both colours]
  B --> C{Any of MY pieces loose}
  C -->|yes| D[Defend it or move it before it is hit]
  C -->|no| E{Two ENEMY pieces loose}
  E -->|yes| F[Find a square or check that hits both]
  E -->|no| G{One enemy piece loose}
  G -->|yes| H[Look for a check that also wins it]
  G -->|no| I[No LPDO target this move]
```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — defend your own loose piece first

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . b . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . B . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Both bishops sit loose on the a1–h8 / a3–f8 diagonals. Whoever moves can hunt — so check your own first.*
**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/2b5/8/8/5PPP/2B3K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/8/2b5/8/8/5PPP/2B3K1_w_-_-_0_1)

Here neither side has an immediate double attack, but the lesson is the **scan**: both bishops are loose, so a single tempo (a future queen or rook arriving with a threat on c5 or c1) could win one. The habit of noticing this *before* it becomes tactical is what prevents blunders.

### Example 2 — loose piece + check

```
8 . . . . k . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . n . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . Q . . .
1 . . . . K . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The knight on b5 is loose, and the black king is exposed — combine them.*
**FEN:** 4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Qh5+!** checks the king along the h5–e8 diagonal **and** attacks the loose knight on b5 along the fifth rank. Black must answer the check; then **2.Qxb5** scoops the knight. The loose piece plus a forcing check is the bread-and-butter winning pattern.

## Common mistakes

- Leaving a piece undefended "just for a moment" — that moment is exactly when the fork lands.
- Counting a piece as defended when its defender is **pinned**, **overloaded**, or about to be **deflected**.
- Only scanning the opponent's loose pieces and not your own.
- Trading into a position where your remaining pieces are scattered and loose.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle theme:** [Hanging piece](https://lichess.org/training/hangingPiece). Do **15–20 per day**.
- **Daily 30-second scan drill:** load any middlegame position and, before reading the solution, name every undefended piece on the board.
- **In your own games:** the blunder-check before every move is simply *"Is anything of mine loose, and can it be hit?"* See [the thinking process](../flowcharts/the-thinking-process.md).

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `2r3k1/6pp/8/8/8/7r/6PP/Q5K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a rook.
2. **FEN:** `6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a minor piece.
3. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the knight with a check.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Qc3!** double-attacks the rook on c8 (c-file) and the rook on h3 (third rank). Black saves one; White takes the other, winning a full rook.
2. **1.Qb4!** attacks the bishop on b6 and the knight on e4. After Black moves one, e.g. **1...Bd8 2.Qxe4**, White wins a clean piece.
3. **1.Qh5+!** checks the king on e8 and attacks the loose knight on b5. After the king moves, **2.Qxb5** wins the knight.

</details>

## Related

- [Fork](fork.md) and [Double attack](double-attack.md) — the tactics loose pieces enable.
- [Overloading](overloading.md) and [Deflection](deflection.md) — how a "defended" piece becomes loose.
- [Pin](pin.md) — a pinned defender means the piece it guards is effectively loose.

Concept tags: [[loose-pieces]] [[lpdo]] [[double-attack]] [[fork]] [[hanging-piece]]

# Loose Pieces (LPDO — "Loose Pieces Drop Off")

> A loose piece is any piece that is undefended (or under-defended). Loose pieces are the raw material of almost every tactic — they are what forks, double attacks, pins and skewers feed on.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
**Prerequisites:** none

## The idea

Most tactics do not appear out of nowhere — they appear because a piece is **undefended**. GM John Nunn coined the rule **LPDO: Loose Pieces Drop Off.** If two of your pieces are loose, the opponent only needs one move that attacks both, and one of them falls.

So strong players develop a constant background habit: every move, they scan the board and ask, *"Which pieces — mine and my opponent's — are undefended?"* Those squares are where double attacks, forks and skewers will land. Reduce your own loose pieces and you stop walking into tactics; spot the opponent's and you start finding them.

## Why it matters

Below master level, the single biggest source of swings is a loose piece getting hit by a fork or a queen double-attack. You do not even need a forcing combination — often a centralizing queen move attacks two undefended pieces at once and wins one for free. Training "loose-piece vision" is the cheapest way to both **stop blundering** and **start winning material**.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . k . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 r . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . Q . . .
1 . . . . K . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Black's rook on a4 is loose and the king is exposed. White hits both with one move.*
**FEN:** 4k3/8/8/8/r7/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/8/8/r7/8/4Q3/4K3_w_-_-_0_1)

White plays **1.Qb5+!** The queen checks the king along the b5–e8 diagonal and, from b5, also attacks the loose rook on a4. Crucially the attack on the rook comes **along a diagonal**, so the rook cannot capture the queen back, and because the move is also check Black has no time to rescue it: **1...Ke7 2.Qxa4** wins the rook clean. One move, a check plus a loose piece — game over.

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . . p p
6 . b . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . n . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . Q . . P P
1 . . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Black's bishop (b6) and knight (e4) are both undefended. The queen forks them.*
**FEN:** 6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Qb4!** attacks the bishop on **b6** (up the b-file) and the knight on **e4** (along the fourth rank). Neither minor piece is defended, so after Black rescues one — **1...Bd8 2.Qxe4** — White wins a clean piece. The combination exists *only* because both minors were loose; defend either one and there is no tactic.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Two loose pieces on a line:** a queen, rook or bishop attacks both at once (a [double attack](double-attack.md) / [fork](fork.md)).
- **Loose piece + check:** the most lethal pattern — a check the opponent *must* answer, and the second move grabs the loose piece with tempo.
- **Loose piece + mate threat:** the threat forces a defensive move; meanwhile the loose piece hangs.
- **Under-defended (not fully loose):** a piece defended once but attacked twice — pile up another attacker.
- **Tactically loose:** a piece "defended" by a pinned, overloaded, or about-to-be-deflected guard is effectively loose. See [overloading](overloading.md) and [deflection](deflection.md).

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[After every opponent move] --> B[List all undefended pieces both colours]
  B --> C{Any of MY pieces loose}
  C -->|yes| D[Defend it or move it before it is hit]
  C -->|no| E{Two ENEMY pieces loose}
  E -->|yes| F[Find a square or check that hits both]
  E -->|no| G{One enemy piece loose}
  G -->|yes| H[Look for a check that also wins it]
  G -->|no| I[No LPDO target this move]
```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — defend your own loose piece first

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . b . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . . B . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Both bishops sit loose on the a1–h8 / a3–f8 diagonals. Whoever moves can hunt — so check your own first.*
**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/2b5/8/8/5PPP/2B3K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/8/2b5/8/8/5PPP/2B3K1_w_-_-_0_1)

Here neither side has an immediate double attack, but the lesson is the **scan**: both bishops are loose, so a single tempo (a future queen or rook arriving with a threat on c5 or c1) could win one. The habit of noticing this *before* it becomes tactical is what prevents blunders.

### Example 2 — loose piece + check

```
8 . . . . k . . .
7 . . . . . . . .
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . n . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . . Q . . .
1 . . . . K . . .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*The knight on b5 is loose, and the black king is exposed — combine them.*
**FEN:** 4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Qh5+!** checks the king along the h5–e8 diagonal **and** attacks the loose knight on b5 along the fifth rank. Black must answer the check; then **2.Qxb5** scoops the knight. The loose piece plus a forcing check is the bread-and-butter winning pattern.

## Common mistakes

- Leaving a piece undefended "just for a moment" — that moment is exactly when the fork lands.
- Counting a piece as defended when its defender is **pinned**, **overloaded**, or about to be **deflected**.
- Only scanning the opponent's loose pieces and not your own.
- Trading into a position where your remaining pieces are scattered and loose.

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle theme:** [Hanging piece](https://lichess.org/training/hangingPiece). Do **15–20 per day**.
- **Daily 30-second scan drill:** load any middlegame position and, before reading the solution, name every undefended piece on the board.
- **In your own games:** the blunder-check before every move is simply *"Is anything of mine loose, and can it be hit?"* See [the thinking process](../flowcharts/the-thinking-process.md).

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/8/r7/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the rook with a check.
2. **FEN:** `6k1/6pp/1b6/8/4n3/8/3Q2PP/6K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win a minor piece.
3. **FEN:** `4k3/8/8/1n6/8/8/4Q3/4K3 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win the knight with a check.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Qc3!** double-attacks the rook on c8 (c-file) and the rook on h3 (third rank). Black saves one; White takes the other, winning a full rook.
2. **1.Qb4!** attacks the bishop on b6 and the knight on e4. After Black moves one, e.g. **1...Bd8 2.Qxe4**, White wins a clean piece.
3. **1.Qh5+!** checks the king on e8 and attacks the loose knight on b5. After the king moves, **2.Qxb5** wins the knight.

</details>

## Related

- [Fork](fork.md) and [Double attack](double-attack.md) — the tactics loose pieces enable.
- [Overloading](overloading.md) and [Deflection](deflection.md) — how a "defended" piece becomes loose.
- [Pin](pin.md) — a pinned defender means the piece it guards is effectively loose.

Concept tags: [[loose-pieces]] [[lpdo]] [[double-attack]] [[fork]] [[hanging-piece]]

# Batteries (Doubling Up on a Line)

> A battery is two (or more) pieces lined up on the same file, rank or diagonal so they reinforce each other's attack along that line.

**Rating focus:** 1500–2100
**Prerequisites:** [Open and half-open files](../04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/open-and-half-open-files.md)

## The idea

One rook on an open file is useful; **two** rooks stacked on it are overwhelming. A battery concentrates force on a single line: the front piece does the attacking, the rear piece supports it, so the front piece can crash into a defended square and the rear piece recaptures. The three classic batteries are:

- **Doubled rooks** (or rook + queen) on a file or rank.
- **Queen + bishop** on a diagonal — the front bishop, queen behind, aiming at a target like h7.
- **Queen + rook** on a file, often invading the 7th/8th rank.

A subtlety worth knowing: on a **diagonal battery**, which piece goes in front matters. Putting the **bishop in front** and the queen behind keeps your queen safe and lets the bishop be the "expendable" spearhead; this is the formation behind the [Greek Gift](../07-Attacking-Play/the-greek-gift-bxh7.md) and many kingside attacks.

## Why it matters

Whole games are won by **seizing an open file with a battery** and invading the back two ranks. And the most common kingside mating attacks are built on a queen-and-bishop battery pointed at the castled king's h7 (or h2) square. Recognizing where to build a battery — and where the opponent is building one against you — is core middlegame technique.

## Diagrams

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 . . . R . . . .
1 . . . R . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*A doubled-rook battery on the open d-file. The front rook penetrates with the rear rook backing it up.*
**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R4/3R2K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R4/3R2K1_w_-_-_0_1)

White plays **1.Rd8#** — the front rook (d2) drives down the open file to the back rank and the king, walled in by its own f7/g7/h7 pawns, is mated. Here a single rook delivers the blow, but the **doubled-rook battery is the standard way to dominate an open file**: if Black had a defender on d8, the rear rook on d1 would recapture and keep a rook on the file. Two rooks beat one for control of the only road into the enemy camp.

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . B . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . Q . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*A queen + bishop battery on the b1–h7 diagonal. Bishop in front, queen behind, both bearing down on h7.*
**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/3B4/5PPP/1Q4K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/3B4/5PPP/1Q4K1_w_-_-_0_1)

The bishop on d3 and queen on b1 share the b1–h7 diagonal. White crashes through with **1.Qxh7+!** — and Black **cannot** reply 1...Kxh7, because the bishop on d3 guards h7. The king must run with **1...Kf8**, leaving White a clean pawn up with a shattered enemy king. Add a knight that can reach g5 and this same battery becomes a forced mate (the Greek Gift). The lesson: the bishop in front lets the queen strike a defended square safely.

## Patterns and sub-types

- **Doubled rooks** on a file — the workhorse for invading the 7th/8th rank.
- **Alekhine's gun:** queen *behind* two rooks on a file (rook–rook–queen) — maximum pressure on one point.
- **Queen + bishop diagonal battery** (bishop in front) — the classic kingside attacker (b1–h7 / b8–h2 diagonals).
- **Queen + rook on a file** — invading down the e- or c-file at a weak point.
- **Rank battery** — two rooks (or rook + queen) doubled on the 7th rank, "pigs on the seventh."

## How to spot it

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Look for an open or half open line] --> B{Can I put two pieces on it}
  B -->|yes| C[Build the battery front piece then rear piece]
  B -->|no| D[Pry the line open first with a pawn break]
  C --> E{Is the target square defended}
  E -->|yes| F[Front piece sacrifices rear piece recaptures]
  E -->|no| G[Invade the back ranks and win material]
```

## Worked examples

### Example 1 — doubled rooks invade the seventh

```
8 . . . . . r k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . . . . . .
2 R . . . . P P P
1 R . . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*White owns the open a-file with a doubled-rook battery and breaks in.*
**FEN:** 5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/R4PPP/R5K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/R4PPP/R5K1_w_-_-_0_1)

White plays **1.Ra8** and after **1...Rxa8 2.Rxa8+** White keeps a rook on the open file and reaches the back rank, forcing **2...Rf8 3.Rxf8+ Kxf8** into a winning king-and-pawn ending where White is the one with the active king. The battery let White trade off the only defender of the file and invade.

### Example 2 — the diagonal battery wins a pawn by force

```
8 . . . . . . k .
7 . . . . . p p p
6 . . . . . . . .
5 . . . . . . . .
4 . . . . . . . .
3 . . . B . . . .
2 . . . . . P P P
1 . Q . . . . K .
  a b c d e f g h
```
*Same b1–h7 battery — the concrete gain.*
**FEN:** 6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/3B4/5PPP/1Q4K1 w - - 0 1
Side to move: White.
[Analyze on Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/3B4/5PPP/1Q4K1_w_-_-_0_1)

**1.Qxh7+ Kf8 2.Qh8+** (or simply consolidating a pawn up) — Black could never recapture on h7 because the bishop covered it. This is the engine behind real attacking sacrifices: the battery makes a "defended" square effectively undefended.

## Common mistakes

- Putting the **queen in front** of the bishop on an attacking diagonal — now your most valuable piece is the spearhead and gets hit or traded.
- Building a battery on a file the opponent can simply block or contest with a single rook.
- Doubling rooks one move too slowly, letting the opponent trade off the open file first.
- Ignoring the enemy's battery aimed at your own king (the b1–h7 / b8–h2 diagonal especially).

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** [Attacking f2/f7](https://lichess.org/training) and Greek-Gift motifs; back-rank puzzles for file batteries.
- **Model games:** study any "pigs on the seventh" rook ending and a Greek-Gift attack; note exactly when the battery was built.
- **In your games:** every time a file opens, ask "can I be first to double rooks on it?"

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R4/3R2K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Use the file battery to mate.
2. **FEN:** `6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/3B4/5PPP/1Q4K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win material with the diagonal battery.
3. **FEN:** `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/R4PPP/R5K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Seize the a-file and reach the 8th rank.

<details><summary>Show answers</summary>

1. **1.Rd8#** — the front rook reaches the back rank; the king is mated behind its own pawns.
2. **1.Qxh7+!** Black cannot play 1...Kxh7 because the bishop on d3 defends h7, so after **1...Kf8** White is a clean pawn up with a crushing attack.
3. **1.Ra8 Rxa8 2.Rxa8+** keeps a rook on the open file and invades the 8th rank, winning the endgame thanks to the active rook and king.

</details>

## Related

- [X-ray](x-ray.md) — pieces acting through one another on a line.
- [Open and half-open files](../04-Strategy-and-Positional-Play/open-and-half-open-files.md) — where rook batteries live.
- [The Greek Gift](../07-Attacking-Play/the-greek-gift-bxh7.md) — the diagonal battery in attacking action.
- [Back-rank mate](back-rank-mate.md) — what a file battery often delivers.

Concept tags: [[batteries]] [[doubled-rooks]] [[alekhines-gun]] [[open-files]] [[greek-gift]]

- Ignoring the enemy's battery aimed at your own king (the b1–h7 / b8–h2 diagonal especially).

## Train it

- **Lichess puzzle themes:** [Attacking f2/f7](https://lichess.org/training) and Greek-Gift motifs; back-rank puzzles for file batteries.
- **Model games:** study any "pigs on the seventh" rook ending and a Greek-Gift attack; note exactly when the battery was built.
- **In your games:** every time a file opens, ask "can I be first to double rooks on it?"

## Exercises

1. **FEN:** `6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/3R4/3R2K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Use the file battery to mate.
2. **FEN:** `6k1/5ppp/8/8/8/3B4/5PPP/1Q4K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Win material with the diagonal battery.
3. **FEN:** `5rk1/5ppp/8/8/8/8/R4PPP/R5K1 w - - 0 1` — White to move. Seize the a-file and reach the 8th rank.
